event_blocks.summary: High-pitched synth sounds and a dark, starry background transition to sweeping aerial views of The Rockies, described as the backbone of North America. A male voiceover, accompanied by dramatic orchestral music, highlights their rugged, spectacular, and majestic nature as the camera glides over peaks, glaciers, and valleys. An older scientist examines a sample under a microscope as the voiceover explains geologists are exposing the rocks' secrets, posing questions about sea creature remains found in mountain peaks and the formation and ongoing rise of the Rockies. A view of Earth from space shows North America, overlaid with floating asteroids and scientific equations, as the voiceover speculates about the forces that built the Rockies potentially splitting North America. The music shifts to a driving, action-oriented orchestral piece with the 'NAKED SCIENCE: THE ROCKIES' title card and National Geographic logo appearing. The segment concludes with a panoramic view of the snow-covered Rockies under a partly cloudy sky. The narrator introduces The Rockies as one of Earth's greatest mountain chains, stretching over 2000 miles and forming the continental divide, with numerous peaks over 14,000 feet. The visuals display the majestic Rockies, transitioning to a rotating Earth from space, then close-ups of rock formations, as the narrator explains their creation by powerful geologic forces. A dynamic underwater view of a rocky seabed, a valley with forests and rivers, and a mist-shrouded mountain range with a lake follow, as the narrator states their story spans 200 million years of geological history. The segment opens with a serene shot of a lake reflecting snow-capped mountains in Canada, transitioning to a time-lapse showing the passage of time and dramatic lighting shifts. Upbeat, dramatic electronic music begins as the narrator explains this is a journey into Earth's ancient past. A rotating view of Earth from space shows North and South America, with the narrator stating that 200 million years ago, there were no Rockies and continents looked different, with only one landmass, Pangea. A detailed topographical view of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge appears, with a red line tracing tectonic plate boundaries as a deep, rumbling tearing sound accompanies the description of a rift opening and tearing Pangea apart. The continents are shown in a fiery, molten state illustrating continental drift and plate tectonics, as the narrator explains Pangea broke apart into smaller landmasses, forming North America, concluding that Earth's surface is broken into vast pieces called plates, accompanied by a deep, resonant clang and hum.

Scientist.description: An older man with white hair, wearing a light blue shirt, seated at a desk in a laboratory, using a microscope and interacting with a computer displaying scientific data, visually representing the 'geologists' mentioned by the voiceover who are 'cracking open these rocks to expose their secrets.'

The Rockies.description: A series of snow-covered, jagged mountain peaks, initially reflected in a calm lake under a cloudy sky, then shown from various aerial angles with dynamic lighting and cloud movement. The narrator refers to these as 'the youngest peaks in the United States' and later clarifies that '200 million years ago, there were no Rockies,' providing a historical context to their formation.

NAKED SCIENCE.description: A recurring on-screen graphic in the lower third, featuring a blue and white design with a vertical line and the text 'NAKED SCIENCE' in bold white letters, consistently displayed throughout the segment.

National Geographic Logo.description: A yellow rectangular border appearing in the upper right corner during the title card, associated with the 'NAKED SCIENCE' branding.

Asteroids and Scientific Equations.description: Floating rocky asteroids in space, overlaid with equations such as 'Gravitational potential energy' and 'Tangential acceleration', suggesting a scientific explanation of geological formation.

Earth from Space.description: A rotating view of Earth, initially showing North and South America against a starry backdrop, then zooming to highlight the Mid-Atlantic Ridge and illustrating tectonic plate boundaries with a red line and molten continental representation. The narrator explains this visual journey takes us into 'Earth's ancient past,' revealing how 'continents looked different,' starting as 'one giant landmass, Pangea,' which then 'break[s] apart into many smaller landmasses,' eventually 'forming the present-day continent of North America,' and that 'Earth's surface is broken up into vast pieces called plates.'

Mist-Covered Mountain and Lake.description: A mountain range partially obscured by low-hanging clouds, with a calm lake in the foreground and dense evergreen forests along the shoreline, under an overcast sky, transitioning into a time-lapse with shifting light and moving clouds. The narrator identifies this initial setting as 'this place in Canada.'

Underwater Seabed.description: A rocky ocean floor with a turquoise hue, illuminated by light filtering through water, appearing as the narrator discusses the "200 million years of geological history" of the Rockies.

Tectonic Plate Boundaries.description: A red line tracing the edges of continental plates on a rotating Earth, visually representing the separation and movement of landmasses, followed by a fiery, molten depiction of continents to illustrate geological activity and continental drift. The narrator describes 'a vast rift is opening up, tearing this supercontinent apart,' accompanied by a deep, rumbling tearing sound, and later explicitly states that 'Earth's surface is broken up into vast pieces called plates.'

raw_segments: The segment opens with ethereal, high-pitched synth sounds over a dark, starry background, quickly transitioning to sweeping aerial views of the snow-capped Rocky Mountains under a clear blue sky, showcasing their rugged terrain and vastness. A deep, authoritative male voiceover begins, accompanied by dramatic orchestral music with percussion, declaring, "The Rocky Mountains. The backbone of North America," and describing them as "rugged, spectacular, and majestic." The camera glides over jagged peaks, glaciers, and valleys, before briefly cutting to an older scientist in a lab, examining a sample under a microscope, as the voiceover explains that "Today geologists are cracking open these rocks to expose their secrets." The scene returns to the mountains with dynamic cloud movements and changing light, while the voiceover poses intriguing questions: "How did the remains of sea creatures end up in mountain peaks, thousands of feet in the air?" The view then zooms out to Earth from space, highlighting North America, as the voiceover continues, "When did the Rockies form? Are they still rising today?" A cosmic setting with floating asteroids and scientific equations overlays the visuals, and the voiceover speculates, "And if they are, could the great forces that built the Rockies one day split North America in two?" The music shifts to a driving, action-oriented orchestral piece with strong percussion and brass as a title card for 'NAKED SCIENCE: THE ROCKIES' appears with the National Geographic logo. The segment concludes with a panoramic view of the snow-covered Rockies under a partly cloudy sky, reinforcing the theme of geological and natural wonder, with the intense orchestral music continuing.

The minute opens with sweeping aerial views of The Rockies, showcasing their jagged, snow-covered peaks under a bright blue sky with drifting clouds, as a male narrator introduces them as "one of the greatest mountain chains on Earth." Throughout, a dramatic, suspenseful orchestral score underscores the visuals. The narrator details their immense scale, stretching "over 2000 miles from the northern tip of British Columbia to the Rio Grande in New Mexico," and being "up to 300 miles wide," forming "the continental divide, the great barrier between East and West." The visuals continue to display the majestic Rockies as the narrator highlights that "in Colorado alone, there are 58 mountain peaks over 14,000 feet tall," clarifying this is "around two and a half miles high." The scene then transitions to a rotating Earth from Space, highlighting North and South America, followed by close-ups of reddish-brown rock formations, as the narrator explains that "The Rockies were created by the most powerful geologic forces on Earth." The dramatic music swells. The sequence shifts to a dynamic underwater view of a rocky seabed with a turquoise hue, then to a wide valley with green forests and winding rivers, and finally to a dramatic, mist-shrouded mountain range with a calm lake reflecting the overcast sky. During these transitions, the narrator continues, stating that "Their dramatic story takes us on a journey from north to south through 200 million years of geological history," before the audio cuts off.

The segment opens with a serene, wide-angle shot of a calm lake reflecting snow-capped mountains, identified by a male narrator as 'this place in Canada,' before he mentions 'the youngest peaks in the United States.' This tranquil natural setting, featuring a mist-covered mountain range and dense evergreen forests, transitions into a dynamic time-lapse where the lighting shifts dramatically from cool blues to warm golden hues, suggesting the passage of time from dusk to dawn, with clouds moving swiftly across the sky. Upbeat, slightly dramatic electronic music begins as the narrator explains this is 'a journey that takes us back into the Earth's ancient past.' The scene then shifts to a rotating view of Earth from space, revealing North and South America against a starry backdrop. The narrator states that '200 million years ago, there were no Rockies,' and 'even the continents looked different,' explaining there was 'just one giant landmass, Pangea.' The camera zooms in on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge with a detailed topographical view, as the narrator warns, 'but not for much longer.' The Earth rotates further, and a red line appears, tracing the boundaries of tectonic plates, emphasizing the separation of continents. A deep, rumbling tearing sound accompanies the narrator's description of 'a vast rift is opening up, tearing this supercontinent apart.' The continents are then shown in a fiery, molten state, illustrating continental drift and plate tectonics, as the narrator continues, 'Pangea will break apart into many smaller landmasses,' eventually 'forming the present-day continent of North America.' A deep, resonant clang and hum are heard before the narrator concludes that 'Earth's surface is broken up into vast pieces called plates.'

event_blocks.summary: Earth from space reveals fiery continents and a red line tracing tectonic plate boundaries, as a narrator explains the warm, dense mantle and convection currents moving plates, transitioning to the Sun's turbulent surface where immense tectonic forces tear continents apart and build mountains, accompanied by deep rumbling sounds. An underwater seabed scene shows a dark object moving through murky water, followed by a metallic clang. Dr. Kirk R. Johnson, chief curator of the Denver Museum of Nature & Science, appears outdoors in an arid landscape with red rock formations, explaining that continents crushing together or being hit by oceanic plates create 'planetary traffic accidents' where mountains form. The NAKED SCIENCE graphic appears, and orchestral music begins as aerial views of The Rockies showcase snow-capped peaks, forests, and a turquoise lake, with dynamic clouds. The narrator notes evidence of these forces in Yoho National Park, British Columbia, with final shots focusing on rugged terrain with sedimentary layers, highlighting geological history and erosion under a partly cloudy sky.

Earth from Space.description: A rotating view of Earth showing North and South America, with visible cloud patterns and landmasses, set against a starry background, transitioning to a detailed topographical view of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge and then illustrating tectonic plate boundaries with a red line and molten continental representation, as a deep-voiced male narrator describes the 'warm, dense mantle' and how 'convection currents' slowly move these plates across the Earth's surface.

Mist-Covered Mountain and Lake.description: A mountain range partially obscured by low-hanging clouds, with a calm lake in the foreground and dense evergreen forests along the shoreline, under an overcast sky, transitioning into a time-lapse with shifting light and moving clouds.

The Rockies.description: A series of snow-covered, jagged mountain peaks shown from various aerial angles, with dynamic lighting and cloud movement, emphasizing their scale and natural beauty, initially reflected in a calm lake under a cloudy sky, transitioning into a time-lapse with shifting light and moving clouds. A deep-voiced male narrator identifies them as one of the oldest and most northerly parts of the Rockies, specifically 'Yoho National Park in British Columbia,' while dramatic, orchestral background music plays.

NAKED SCIENCE.description: A recurring on-screen graphic in the lower third, featuring a blue and white design with a vertical line and the text 'NAKED SCIENCE' in bold white letters, consistently displayed throughout the segment, appearing as Dr. Kirk R. Johnson is introduced by the narrator.

Dr. Kirk R. Johnson.description: A man with short brown hair and glasses, wearing a light gray collared shirt, standing outdoors in a rocky, arid landscape with layered red rock formations and sparse vegetation, speaking directly to the camera. A lower-third graphic identifies him as 'Dr. Kirk R. Johnson, Denver Museum of Nature & Science.' He speaks with a clear, articulate voice, explaining that 'the action of continents crushing together or continents being hit by oceanic plates' creates 'basically planetary traffic accidents' where 'the wrecks result in mountains forming.'

Tectonic Plate Boundaries.description: A red line tracing the edges of continental plates on a rotating Earth, visually representing the separation and movement of landmasses, followed by a fiery, molten depiction of continents to illustrate geological activity and continental drift. A deep-voiced male narrator explains that where plates meet, 'immense tectonic forces' can 'tear continents apart' but also 'build mountains,' accompanied by deep, rumbling and thrumming sounds.

Underwater Seabed.description: A rocky ocean floor with a turquoise hue, illuminated by light filtering through water, suggesting a deep-sea environment, where a dark, elongated object moves through murky water, hinting at deep-sea exploration or tectonic activity beneath the ocean, following a segment discussing tectonic forces.

raw_segments: The video opens with a dramatic depiction of Earth from space, showing a fiery, molten representation of the continents, emphasizing tectonic activity and continental drift with a red line tracing plate boundaries, as a deep-voiced male narrator explains the "warm, dense mantle" and how "convection currents" slowly move these plates across the Earth's surface. This transitions into a close-up of the Sun’s turbulent surface, glowing with intense orange and red hues, while the narrator continues, detailing how "immense tectonic forces" at plate edges can "tear continents apart" but also "build mountains," accompanied by deep, rumbling and thrumming sounds. The scene then shifts to an underwater seabed, where a dark, elongated object moves through murky, turquoise water, hinting at deep-sea exploration or tectonic activity beneath the ocean. A distinct, almost metallic clang is heard before the narrative cuts to a man, identified by a lower-third graphic as Dr. Kirk R. Johnson from the Denver Museum of Nature & Science, standing outdoors in a rocky, arid landscape with layered red rock formations and sparse vegetation. The narrator introduces him as "Kirk Johnson, chief curator of the Denver Museum of Nature and Science," who "explains." The NAKED SCIENCE graphic appears. Dr. Kirk R. Johnson, speaking directly to the camera with a clear, articulate voice, explains that "the action of continents crushing together or continents being hit by oceanic plates" creates "basically planetary traffic accidents" where "the wrecks result in mountains forming." Dramatic, orchestral background music begins as the video transitions to sweeping aerial views of The Rockies, showcasing snow-capped peaks, dense evergreen forests, and a vibrant turquoise lake nestled in the valley, with dynamic cloud movements and shifting light. The narrator returns, stating that "evidence of the power of these immense forces can be found in one of the oldest and most northerly parts of the Rockies," specifically "Yoho National Park in British Columbia." The final shots focus on rugged, rocky terrain with visible sedimentary layers, emphasizing geological history and erosion, under a partly cloudy sky.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with close-ups of fossilized marine life, revealing segmented bodies and appendages, identified as thousands of peculiar prehistoric creatures discovered in a cliff face near Mount Stephen, 8,000 feet above sea level. Geologist Paul McNeil, from the Burgess Shale Geoscience Foundation, explains that these unique fossils, found almost nowhere else and unlike any living animal, are preserved with soft tissues and anatomical features, including specimens like Hallucigenia and Wiwaxia. A 3D animated reconstruction depicts an ancient underwater environment on a sandy seabed with tube-like organisms and sponges, illustrating the sea creatures that lived and died over half a billion years ago in a shallow tropical sea. These fossils provide clues to the formation of the early Northern Rockies, with the containing rocks formed on the seafloor. McNeil further clarifies that the surrounding rocks, once an ancient seabed, include limestones from the continental shelf and shales from deeper waters, all originally submerged. Sediment laid down over 8,000 years solidified into rock up to 12 miles thick, now found a mile and a half above sea level, having been buried six miles deep and thrust up over 2 kilometers. This ancient rock formed offshore and was pushed inland and upward by subduction, a process visualized by a 3D animated Earth from space showing tectonic plate boundaries and continental drift. The North American and Pacific Ocean plates collided head-on, with the denser Pacific plate forced down in a "clash of the Titans," explaining seafloor spreading and continental separation.

Paul McNeil.description: A man with short brown hair, wearing a white bucket hat with a dark brim and a dark jacket, speaking directly to the camera in a clear, explanatory male voice, identified as 'Paul McNeil, Burgess Shale Geoscience Foundation'.

3D Animated Prehistoric Marine Life.description: Colorful, computer-generated reconstructions of ancient sea creatures, including purple spiky organisms and tube-like structures, are animated moving across a sandy seabed, visually representing the 'tens of thousands of sea creatures' that, as the narrator describes, were trapped and fossilized in the accumulating sediments of the ancient shallow sea.

Fossil Specimens.description: Fossil specimens, though not directly visible, are strongly implied by the layered rock and animated prehistoric life, and are explicitly mentioned by the narrator as 'tens of thousands of sea creatures' that were trapped and fossilized, now found a mile and a half above sea level but once buried six miles deep.

The Rockies.description: A series of snow-covered, jagged mountain peaks shown from various aerial angles, with dynamic lighting and cloud movement, emphasizing their scale and natural beauty, initially reflected in a calm lake under a cloudy sky, now understood to have formed around the time these ancient sea creatures existed, with their geological history linked to the fossils found there.

NAKED SCIENCE.description: A recurring on-screen graphic in the lower third, featuring a blue and white design with a vertical line and the text 'NAKED SCIENCE' in bold white letters, consistently displayed throughout the segment.

Underwater Seabed.description: The underwater seabed is depicted transitioning from a sunlit, clear turquoise rocky ocean floor to darker, deeper regions, visually representing the ancient marine environment where, as a narrator explains, the West Coast of North America was once covered by a shallow sea, accumulating clay, mud, and gravel that trapped and fossilized countless sea creatures.

Layered Rock Formations.description: A prominent geological feature with distinct horizontal strata in shades of brown, gray, and orange, showing signs of erosion and mineral variation, whose fossils Paul McNeil explains were buried deep and then thrust upwards.

Earth from Space.description: A rotating view of Earth showing North and South America, with visible cloud patterns and landmasses, set against a starry background, transitioning to a detailed topographical view of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge and then illustrating tectonic plate boundaries with a red line and molten continental representation, used to visually explain continental drift, seafloor spreading, and subduction as described by the narrator.

Tectonic Plate Boundaries.description: A red line tracing the edges of continental plates on a rotating Earth, visually representing the separation and movement of landmasses, followed by a fiery, molten depiction of continents to illustrate geological activity and continental drift, which the narrator explains were involved in the "head-on" collision of the North American and Pacific Ocean plates.

raw_segments: The segment opens with dramatic, slightly mysterious background music accompanying close-up, black-and-white images of fossilized ancient marine life, showcasing intricate segmented bodies and appendages. A narrator explains these are thousands of peculiar prehistoric creatures found in a cliff face near Mount Stephen, 8,000 feet above sea level. The scene transitions to a rugged mountainous terrain where researchers, including geologist and mountain guide Paul McNeil from the Burgess Shale Geoscience Foundation, are actively collecting rock samples. Paul, wearing a white bucket hat and dark jacket, holds a fossilized specimen and speaks directly to the camera with a clear American accent, stating that these fossils are found almost nowhere else and are unlike any animal alive today, with the NAKED SCIENCE logo visible. The video returns to detailed fossil close-ups, with the narrator identifying a "spiny creature" as Hallucigenia and a "bizarre specimen" as Wiwaxia, emphasizing their preserved soft tissues and anatomical features. A vibrant 3D animated reconstruction then depicts an ancient underwater environment, showing various prehistoric creatures, confirmed by the narrator as sea creatures, moving among colorful tube-like organisms and sponges on a sandy seabed with rising bubbles. The narrator explains these fossils offer clues to how the early Northern Rockies formed, as they are the remains of sea creatures that lived and died in a shallow tropical sea over half a billion years ago, with the rocks containing them formed on the sea floor. The segment concludes with sweeping aerial shots of snow-capped mountain peaks, highlighting the dramatic geological landscape.

The segment opens with a sweeping view of a rugged, snow-dusted mountain peak under a bright blue sky, framed by evergreen trees, establishing a dramatic natural setting. Paul McNeil, identified as being from the 'Burgess Shale Geoscience Foundation', appears in a close-up, wearing a white bucket hat and dark jacket against layered rock formations. He speaks with a clear, informative voice, explaining that the surrounding rocks were once an ancient seabed, with limestones forming part of the continental shelf and shales from deeper ocean waters, all originally submerged. As orchestral background music begins, the camera pans across a dry, rocky cliff face with distinct horizontal strata, emphasizing geological layering. This transitions to an underwater sequence, showing a sunlit seabed with clear turquoise water moving into darker, deeper ocean depths with rocky terrain. A narrator's authoritative voice explains that long before the Rockies formed, the West Coast of North America was covered by a shallow sea where, over millions of years, clay, mud, and gravel settled on the coastal shelf, trapping and fossilizing tens of thousands of sea creatures. The video returns to the terrestrial rock face, now overlaid with a 3D animated reconstruction of colorful prehistoric marine life, including purple spiky creatures and tube-like organisms, moving across a sandy seabed, visually representing the fossilized creatures. A translucent blue overlay appears, indicating a time span of '8000 YEARS' between two horizontal lines on the rock layers, as the narrator specifies that these rock layers were made from sediment laid down over 8,000 years, solidifying into rock up to 12 miles thick. The segment concludes with detailed close-ups of the layered rock, highlighting textures and mineral variations, reinforcing the theme of deep time and fossil preservation, with the narrator adding that these fossils are now a mile and a half above sea level but were once buried six miles under other layers of rock.

The segment opens with a close-up of Paul McNeil, wearing a white bucket hat with a dark brim and a dark jacket, speaking directly to the camera in a clear, explanatory male voice. He gestures slightly as he explains how fossils in the layered rock formations behind him were buried over 10 kilometers deep under the Earth's crust and then thrust up over 2 kilometers. A dramatic, resonant sound effect introduces a camera pan across the detailed, stratified rock face, highlighting its horizontal layers in shades of brown, gray, and orange, with visible erosion and mineral variation. The view then expands to a wider landscape showing a rugged, rocky slope with distant snow-capped peaks under a cloudy sky, emphasizing the mountainous terrain. A narrator, with a calm, authoritative male voice, begins to speak, explaining that this ancient rock formed offshore and was pushed inland and upward by a powerful force called "subduction," as subtle, suspenseful background music starts. The scene transitions to a 3D animated Earth from space, rotating to show North and South America, with a red line tracing tectonic plate boundaries. Animated arrows appear, illustrating continental drift and plate movement, as the narrator details how 180 million years ago, the North American plate was moving west and the Pacific Ocean plate was heading east, leading to a "head-on" collision. The animation then shifts to a cross-sectional view of the Earth’s crust, showing two tectonic plates moving apart over a molten mantle, visually explaining the process of seafloor spreading and continental separation, with the narrator describing the denser Pacific Ocean plate being forced down in this "clash of the Titans," all underscored by building, dramatic music and sound effects.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a 3D animated cross-section of Earth's crust, illustrating tectonic plates moving over a molten core, with arrows indicating subduction and continental plates diverging, forming rifts that fill with sediment to create new landmasses. A narrator explains how offshore islands were pushed eastward like a conveyor belt, slamming into the continental shelf, and how the seabed folded like a crumpled carpet, pushing fossils of the Burgess Shale inland and upward. This geological process is visually linked to the formation of the Rockies in British Columbia. Geologist and author Ben Gadd, identified by a 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo, appears in a mountainous landscape, explaining the 'pushing and shoving business' of geological forces. He uses his hands as an analogy for continental plates and sedimentary layers, describing how the 'skin' wrinkles to form mountains, humorously noting, 'The older I get, the better this works.' The animation then depicts two continental plates diverging, causing a body of water to recede and a rift to fill with sediment, with the narrator stating that plate collision forced up the Northern Rockies, and a second collision occurred 80 million years later. The process of 'bulldozing everything eastward and upward' is shown, with 'layer after layer of rock shoved higher,' forming hills and mountains. Aerial and satellite views highlight the western coast of North America and the evolution of gentle folds into the mighty Rockies, with terrains making up British Columbia. The narrator emphasizes that as the Rockies rose, erosion began to tear them down, with dramatic mountain ranges shown at sunset and in darker, shadowed views. The narrative returns to the Rockies, described as 'Jagged peaks, steep cliffs, and razor-sharp ridges carved from the sedimentary rock of an ancient seabed,' with layers of rock still lying almost horizontally, providing a clue to their rise. The animation of tectonic plates diverging and sediment filling a rift visually explains mountain formation, with the narrator detailing how slabs of sedimentary rock were pressurized, folded, and snapped, forming 'broken sheets of rock' that slid over each other in a process called thrust faulting. Ben Gadd further explains thrust faulting, a classic feature of the Canadian Rockies, using his book, 'The Book of the Canadian Rockies,' as a visual aid. The animation then models thrust faulting with colored sand, showing older rock layers pushed above younger ones to create saw-tooth ranges, with the mountains first emerging 140 million years ago and the front ranges appearing 85 million years ago. The narrative shifts to the slowing and eventual stopping of thrust faulting 50 million years ago, with Mount Yamnuska identified as the meeting point of mountains and foothills. Immense forces are described as having pushed an ancient seabed thousands of feet into the air, and erosion is introduced as a force that sculpted the peaks. Evidence of erosion, such as 'piles of broken shale,' is noted. Ben Gadd explains that erosion has been as important as mountain building, with water loosening material, dissolving minerals, and freezing to break rocks. The narrator states that erosion has scraped up to two miles of rock off the mountain peaks, and the Northern Rockies are revealed to be made of sedimentary rock like sandstone and shale.

Viscous Liquid Container.description: A shallow, rectangular white plastic container filled with a dark, thick, tar-like liquid, referred to by Prof. Geissman as 'oil,' used to demonstrate the slow sinking of a CD, symbolizing geological time scales.

CD in Viscous Liquid.description: A standard silver CD placed into a shallow white container filled with a dark, thick liquid, referred to by Prof. Geissman as 'oil,' used to demonstrate slow sinking and resistance, mirroring geological processes. Prof. Geissman describes the 'oil' migrating over the CD and the CD itself moving 'into the oil' as a 'subduction process'.

NAKED SCIENCE.description: A recurring on-screen graphic in the lower third, featuring a blue and white design with a vertical line and the text 'NAKED SCIENCE' in bold white letters, consistently displayed during the field segment, identifying the program's branding.

Tectonic Plate Boundaries.description: A 3D animated cross-section showing a red block (continental plate) moving horizontally over a molten core, with a white scale bar and decreasing numerical values (e.g., 139 to 109) indicating geological progression, overlaid on real mountain footage.

Prof. John W. Geissman.description: A man with graying hair and a mustache, wearing a yellow checkered short-sleeve shirt, standing in an office with bookshelves and a computer. He is introduced by a male narrator as 'Professor John Geissman from the University of New Mexico' and speaks throughout the demonstration with a clear, academic, and enthusiastic tone, explaining the process of 'subduction' using a CD and viscous liquid.

Ben Gar.description: An older man with a white beard, wearing a green cap and a light-colored jacket, holding a rock and speaking directly to the camera in a mountainous outdoor setting, with the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo identifying him as 'Ben Gar, Rockies' in the lower third.

The Rockies.description: A real-world mountain landscape with snow-capped peaks, layered rock formations, and dynamic lighting, serving as the backdrop for the field segment and intercut with the tectonic animation, showcasing both rugged cliffs and forested valleys, specifically noted by a narrator to be made of sedimentary rock like sandstone and shale.

Ben Gadd.description: An older man with a white beard, wearing a green cap with a mountain logo, a light beige jacket over a brown sweater, and khaki pants, speaking directly to the camera in a mountainous outdoor setting, gesturing occasionally as he explains geological concepts in a clear, slightly older, authoritative yet conversational voice, discussing the importance of erosion, mountain building processes, and the role of water in shaping the landscape.

Mist-Covered Mountain and Lake.description: A mountain range partially obscured by low-hanging clouds, with a calm lake in the foreground and dense evergreen forests along the shoreline, under an overcast sky, transitioning into a time-lapse with shifting light and moving clouds.

Earth from Space.description: A rotating view of Earth showing North and South America, with visible cloud patterns and landmasses, set against a starry background, transitioning to a detailed topographical view of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge and then illustrating tectonic plate boundaries with a red line and molten continental representation. This view highlights the western coast of North America as the narrator mentions 'slabs of land, called terrains,' making up 'British Columbia.'

Layered Rock Formations.description: A prominent geological feature with distinct horizontal strata in shades of brown, gray, and orange, showing signs of erosion and mineral variation, visible both in real footage and animated form. A male narrator identifies 'erosion' as the 'very different force' that 'sculpted it into these striking peaks,' noting that 'evidence of erosion's power is strewn all over the mountainside,' specifically mentioning 'piles of broken shale.'

Book: The Book of the Canadian Rockies.description: A physical book with a blue cover featuring a mountain image and the title 'The Book of the Canadian Rockies' in white and yellow text, authored by Ben Gadd, shown resting on a rock and being flipped through by Ben Gadd.

Tectonic Plate Animation Overlay.description: A 3D animated cross-section of Earth's crust showing a horizontal block of layered rock being compressed and folded, illustrating the geological process of mountain formation, overlaid on real mountain footage to provide scientific context.

3D Animated Prehistoric Marine Life.description: Not visible in this segment; replaced by a 3D animated cross-section of tectonic plates.

raw_segments: The minute opens with a 3D animated cross-section of Earth's crust, depicting tectonic plates moving over a molten core with arrows indicating subduction, as a male narrator explains how one plate moves "beneath the more buoyant North American plate," accompanied by dramatic, suspenseful music. The scene quickly transitions through a blurred, fast-moving interior shot before settling on Prof. John W. Geissman, identified by a lower-third graphic from 'NAKED SCIENCE', standing in a well-lit office. He begins a demonstration, placing a standard silver CD into a shallow white container filled with a dark, viscous liquid. Speaking with a clear, academic, and enthusiastic tone, he explains, "What I'm going to do is simply tap on one of the sides and I'm going to create an instability." He gently presses the CD, which floats and slowly sinks. As the camera zooms on the submerging CD, then cuts to a close-up of Prof. Geissman, he continues, "Now we have oil starting to migrate, move its way over the top of the CD. Ah! Voila, it's happening all by itself. I don't have to nudge it anymore." He describes the "oil moving its way across the CD" and the CD itself beginning to move "into the oil," completing "the subduction process." Returning to the partially submerged CD, he emphasizes, "It's subducted into the interior of our planet, if you will. And it's a runaway process. You can't stop it." The segment concludes with a return to the animated tectonic plate movement, mirroring the opening, as the dramatic music swells again.

The minute opens with a dramatic, slightly ominous background score, punctuated by rumbling and scraping sound effects, as a 3D animated cross-section of Earth's crust illustrates tectonic plate boundaries. Yellow arrows indicate two continental plates moving horizontally over a molten core. A male narrator explains that offshore islands on the subducting Pacific plate were pushed eastward like a conveyor belt, eventually reaching and slamming into the continental shelf on the western edge of North America. As the plates diverge, a body of water recedes, revealing a widening rift that eventually fills with sediment, forming a new landmass. The narrator details how the seabed was shoved eastward, slowly folding up like a crumpled carpet, pushing the fossils of the Burgess Shale thousands of feet into the air and miles inland. The animation then transitions to a real-world view of a rugged mountain landscape with snow-capped peaks and dense forests under a partly cloudy sky, which the narrator identifies as the Rockies in British Columbia. The scene cuts to a close-up of geologist and author Ben Gad, an older man with a white beard and a green cap, holding a rock and speaking directly to the camera. The 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo appears in the lower third, identifying him. Ben Gad, whose clear and informative voice is now heard, gestures with the rock, emphasizing his explanation as he begins to describe the "pushing and shoving business" of geological forces, set against the scenic mountain backdrop.

The video opens with Ben Gadd, identified by a lower-third graphic as 'Ben Gadd, Rockies Geologist and Author' under the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo, standing in a mountainous outdoor environment with snow-capped peaks and evergreen trees under a partly cloudy sky. With a full white beard, wearing a green cap and light beige jacket, he gestures with his hands, which the camera zooms in on, to explain geological concepts. He uses his hands as an analogy, describing the "bony part" as a continental plate and the "skin" as sedimentary layers, explaining in a clear, explanatory voice how the "skin" wrinkles up when pushed along the "bony layer," forming mountains. He concludes his explanation by humorously remarking, "The older I get, the better this works for some reason." The scene then transitions to a 3D animated sequence illustrating tectonic plate boundaries. Two continental plates are shown moving horizontally over a glowing orange-yellow molten core, with yellow arrows indicating their divergence. A body of water between them recedes, forming a rift that gradually fills with sediment, visually representing continental rifting and seafloor spreading. A male narrator with a deep, authoritative voice, accompanied by dramatic, building background music and sound effects, explains that "Over millions of years, the plate collision forced up the Northern Rockies." He continues, stating that when the collision's force diminished, the first wave of mountain building ended, but "80 million years after the first block of land hit the coast, a second one slammed into its back," hinting at further geological events.

The segment opens with a dramatic, orchestral score as a 3D animated cross-section of Earth's crust illustrates tectonic plate boundaries. A narrator explains how the process "bulldozed everything eastward and upward," with "layer after layer of rock was shoved higher and higher," forming "hills and then into mountains." This visual depicts two continental plates diverging over a molten core, causing a body of water to recede and a rift to form, which gradually fills with sediment. The animation transitions to a real-world aerial view of a lush, green mountainous region with lakes and forests, followed by a satellite view of Earth highlighting the western coast of North America in green, as the narrator describes how "gentle folds of rock evolved into the mighty Rockies." The scene returns to the 3D animation, showing the plates continuing to diverge and sediment accumulating. The narrator continues, stating that "Today these slabs of land, called terrains, make up British Columbia." Breathtaking real-world footage then showcases snow-covered mountain peaks under a clear blue sky, with the camera gliding through narrow valleys and over glaciers, emphasizing the rugged terrain. The narrator explains that "As they continued to rise, the Rockies were continually sculpted by the momentous force of the elements," adding that "even as they were pushed up, erosion was starting to tear them down." The final scenes show a dramatic mountain range at sunset, with golden light illuminating rocky faces above a dense forest, transitioning to a darker, more shadowed view as clouds drift across the peaks, underscoring the dynamic and majestic nature of mountain landscapes, concluding with the narrator identifying them as "The Canadian Rockies."

The video opens with sweeping panoramic shots of the rugged mountain range, identified as The Rockies, showcasing steep, rocky peaks with patches of snow under a clear blue sky. A narrator describes these as "Jagged peaks, steep cliffs, and razor-sharp ridges carved from the sedimentary rock of an ancient seabed," accompanied by dramatic background music. The camera pans across the landscape, revealing dense evergreen forests and dramatic rock faces illuminated by golden sunlight. The narrator explains that "Despite being caught in a mighty plate collision and thrust up into towering peaks, the layers of rock in these mountains still lie almost horizontally." This visual explanation transitions into a 3D animated cross-section of Earth's crust, illustrating tectonic plate boundaries with two continental plates moving horizontally over a molten core, yellow arrows indicating direction, and a body of water receding as the plates diverge, forming a rift. The narrator states, "This provides a clue about how they rose up." The animation shows the rift gradually filling with sediment, visually explaining the geological formation of mountain ranges. The narrator elaborates, "As the blocks of land drove into the coast of North America, slabs of sedimentary rock were pressurized and folded so much that they snapped," punctuated by a sharp snapping sound effect. The animation then transitions back to real-world footage of the same mountainous terrain, now under a partly cloudy sky with pink-tinged clouds, emphasizing the layered rock formations and the dynamic interplay of light and shadow across the peaks. The narrator concludes, "Broken sheets of rock, up to a mile thick, shunted forwards and slid over each other again and again, in a process scientists call thrust faulting," as the dramatic music swells.

The segment opens with Ben Gadd, a Rockies Geologist and Author, in a sunlit, rocky mountainous environment, wearing a green cap and beige jacket. The 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo appears as he speaks directly to the camera in a clear, explanatory voice, gesturing to describe how rock can be folded to a breaking point, leading to what he calls a 'thrust fault,' a classic feature of the Canadian Rockies. A consistent background music track begins and continues throughout. The scene then transitions to breathtaking panoramic shots of a serene alpine lake surrounded by towering, snow-capped mountains, with mist drifting over peaks and vibrant yellow wildflowers in the foreground. A distinct narrator's voice explains that while sedimentary rock usually forms with younger layers on top of older ones, in the Rockies, thrust faulting has pushed older layers up and over younger ones, creating jumbled formations. The video cuts back to Ben Gadd, now seated on a rocky outcrop, holding his book, 'The Book of the Canadian Rockies'. He places the book on a rock and flips through its pages, using it as a visual aid to further illustrate how a thrust fault causes one part of the rock to break and slide a long way, until it's eventually stopped.

The segment opens with Ben Gadd, a geologist and author, seated on a rocky outcrop in a rugged mountain environment. Wearing a green cap with a mountain logo, a light beige jacket over a brown sweater, and khaki pants, he gestures toward 'The Book of the Canadian Rockies' resting beside him, which features a blue cover with a mountain image and white and yellow text. Gadd explains in a clear, instructional tone, "So now we have layers that were down here, pushed up over these layers. Old rock pushed up and over younger rock," as he flips through the book's pages, emphasizing the geological concepts. Upbeat, slightly dramatic instrumental music begins as the scene transitions to a 3D animated cross-section of tectonic plates. A narrator's voice takes over, explaining, "In this laboratory sandbox, the layers of colored sand represent layers of sedimentary rock. As the sand pushes eastward to the right, thrust faults form, one in front of the other." The animation shows a red block, representing a continental plate, moving horizontally over a molten core, with a white scale bar and numerical values decreasing (e.g., 139, 132, 124, down to 109), illustrating geological compression or convergence. This animation is overlaid on real footage of snow-capped peaks and layered rock formations, with dynamic lighting shifts from bright sunlight to cloud-dappled skies. The narrator continues, "Thrust faulting pushes older rock layers above younger, to create the distinctive saw-tooth ranges of the northern Rockies." The animation, intercut with wide shots of snow-covered peaks and close-ups of layered rock, visually reinforces the connection between the model and actual geology. The narrator details the timeline: "These mountains first emerged 140 million years ago. Thrust faulting first built the western ranges, and then crept eastward, forming a new range further inland. 120 million years ago, the main ranges appeared. And then even further east, the front ranges, 85 million years ago." The segment concludes with the animation showing the red block nearly fully advanced, with the number '109' displayed, as the background shifts to a sunlit mountain face with visible rock layers and snow patches, symbolizing the culmination of tectonic forces shaping the Rockies.

The segment opens with a dramatic, slightly ominous orchestral score accompanying sweeping aerial views of the Rocky Mountains, showcasing winding roads through dense evergreen forests, towering peaks, and majestic glaciers with rivers flowing through lush valleys. As a 3D animated cross-section of tectonic plates appears, illustrating a red continental block moving over a molten core with a decreasing scale bar (69 to 55), a male narrator explains that "Thrust faulting began to slow down until eventually, 50 million years ago, it stopped." The visuals transition to panoramic aerials and satellite views of rugged mountain ridges and snow-covered peaks, while the narrator identifies "Mount Yamnuska" as the point where "the mountains and the foothills meet," bordering "the flat plains of Alberta and Montana." The music swells with a melancholic string melody as close-ups of rocky slopes and layered mountain faces appear, and the narrator describes how "immense forces pushed an ancient seabed thousands of feet into the air." The music continues with the melancholic string as the narrator introduces "a very different force" that "sculpted it into these striking peaks: Erosion." As the camera focuses on fractured, layered rock formations, the narrator emphasizes that "Evidence of erosion's power is strewn all over the mountainside," with the music shifting to a more driving, percussive tone. The segment concludes with a serene river landscape as the narrator mentions "piles of broken shale," linking the visual evidence of erosion to specific geological features.

The segment opens with sweeping aerial shots of rugged, snow-dusted mountain terrain, showcasing steep rock faces and glacial valleys under bright sunlight, establishing the grandeur of The Rockies, accompanied by light, slightly dramatic orchestral background music. A male narrator's voice describes "heaps of ash-colored sediment called scree." The scene transitions to a close-up of Ben Gadd, an older man with a white beard, wearing a green cap and beige jacket, speaking directly to the camera against a backdrop of distant snow-capped peaks. In a clear, slightly older, authoritative yet conversational voice, Ben Gadd explains that "Erosion has been at least as important as the mountain building itself," detailing how the up-piling gives elevation, but the landscape is then carved downward differentially. The narrative shifts to a 3D animated cross-section illustrating tectonic plate movement, where a horizontal block of layered rock is compressed and folded, visually explaining mountain formation. This animation transitions into real-world footage of majestic, layered rock formations in The Rockies, with sunlight casting dynamic shadows. The camera pans across the intricate strata and forested slopes, cutting to a cascading waterfall, emphasizing erosional forces. The male narrator returns, stating that "Erosion is so powerful that it has scraped up to two miles of solid rock off the top of these mountain peaks." Ben Gadd then continues his explanation, intercut with wide-angle views of the mountain range, describing how "Water loosens material, sends it downhill. Water dissolves minerals. Water freezes and breaks up the rocks." The sequence concludes with a panoramic shot of a valley flanked by towering peaks, with a winding river and trail, as the narrator reveals that "The Northern Rockies are made of sedimentary rock like sandstone, shale..." The background music maintains a thoughtful, sometimes epic, orchestral tone throughout.

event_blocks.summary: The Rockies' dramatic time-lapse reveals snow-capped peaks under a turbulent sky, with clouds swirling around rugged, rocky summits, as a narrator explains how soft and soluble limestone is "easy prey for erosion." Transitioning to a wide-angle view of a vast, barren glacier in Canada, small groups of hikers in colorful outdoor gear walk along marked paths, emphasizing the landscape's scale. The camera pans across the glacier, revealing its cracked, textured surface and surrounding steep, rocky slopes, while the narrator details how "in this cold climate, snowfall freezes and compacts to form enormous sheets of ice: glaciers." A closer shot highlights the intricate blue and white patterns of the ice with a large boulder, before an aerial view showcases the immense size of the deep glacial valley. A group observes a meltwater stream, as the narration reveals these glaciers "creep forward at 50 feet per year," which "on a geologic time scale, this is fast." The final frames return to a wide shot of the glacier with scattered hikers under a dynamic sky, reinforcing the grandeur and isolation, as the narrator concludes by describing how glaciers "pick up and carry loose rocks and boulders, which scour and scratch the rock beneath them," like "giant ice cream scoops."

The Rockies.description: A real-world mountain landscape in Canada, visually characterized by snow-capped peaks, layered rock formations, and dynamic lighting. The accompanying narration describes how its soft limestone is susceptible to erosion, and how its cold climate leads to the formation of enormous glaciers that creep forward at 50 feet per year, picking up and scouring rocks beneath them, illustrating the active geological processes shaping this environment.

event_blocks.summary: The Rockies, a rugged mountain landscape of steep, forested cliffs and deep valleys with winding rivers under bright blue skies, are characterized by U-shaped valleys, deep gorges, and steep-sided peaks, sculpted by glaciers and millions of years of erosion. Despite erosion attacking their summits, the mountains are still growing from deep below ground, with roots extending significantly deeper than their visible height, acting as a secret defense. This geological complexity is revealed through sweeping aerial views, dynamic camera movements over snow-dusted peaks, vast glaciers, snowfields, jagged ridges, turquoise alpine lakes, and cascading waterfalls, all bathed in the golden light of sunrise or sunset illuminating intricate rock formations and casting long shadows.

The Rockies.description: A vast mountain landscape featuring snow-capped peaks, deep valleys, glaciers, alpine lakes, and layered rock formations, captured through dynamic aerial and panoramic shots that emphasize geological scale and natural beauty under changing light conditions. A narrator describes its formation through glacial gouging and erosion over millions of years, its ongoing growth deep underground, and its extensive hidden root system, which acts as a 'secret defense' and can extend many times deeper than the visible height of the mountains, likened to the submerged portion of an iceberg.

raw_segments: The video opens with a sweeping aerial view of a rugged mountain landscape, showcasing steep, forested cliffs and a deep valley with a winding river below, under a bright blue sky. A narrator explains that glaciers gouge out distinctive U-shaped valleys, and when they melt, they leave behind deep gorges and steep-sided peaks. As dramatic, slightly ominous background music begins, the camera glides along a rocky precipice, revealing expansive views of snow-dusted peaks. The narrator continues, stating that erosion has worn down these rocks over millions of years, but "The Rockies are fighting back." The perspective shifts to higher altitudes, revealing vast glaciers and snowfields nestled between jagged mountain ridges, with the camera moving dynamically over icy terrain. The narration clarifies that while erosion attacks their summits, deep below ground, the mountains are still growing. The scene transitions to a broader panoramic view of a turquoise alpine lake surrounded by towering peaks, followed by a descent into a wide, gravelly riverbed with cascading waterfalls. The narrator likens looking at The Rockies to seeing the tip of an iceberg, explaining that mountains have roots five or six times deeper than their height, with a two-mile-high mountain potentially having roots extending 13 miles underground. The final moments capture the golden light of sunrise or sunset illuminating the layered rock formations, casting long shadows and highlighting the intricate textures and geological complexity, as the narrator concludes that these roots are The Rockies' secret defense. The entire sequence is devoid of human presence, focusing purely on the natural grandeur.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins with aerial views of sunlit mountain ranges, accompanied by instrumental music. Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom is introduced as a structural geologist from the University of New Mexico. He explains, using blocks of ice, how red and green layers represent mountains above ground, and white layers represent their roots. The scene shifts to an Outdoor Experiment Setup where Karlstrom, now wearing an orange polo shirt, places two White Foam Blocks into a Glass Tank with Water, demonstrating buoyancy and displacement, creating ripples and bubbles. Identified on-screen with the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo, Karlstrom explains that mountains are not static and are continually reshaped by erosion. He then removes one of the White Foam Blocks, explaining how the surface has lowered and rock deep in the earth has come to the surface, a process he calls 'rock uplift.' He then removes another layer, describing a 'continent without a major mountain belt,' all while the background music continues.

Glass Tank with Water.description: A clear rectangular glass tank filled with water, placed on a stone surface outdoors, used to demonstrate buoyancy with white foam blocks, showing ripples and bubbles as the blocks are manipulated.

While visually foam, the accompanying audio narration describes the demonstration conceptually using 'blocks of ice' representing mountains and their roots, and Prof. Karlstrom refers to 'blocks' and 'layers' in his explanation of erosion and rock uplift.

Outdoor Experiment Setup.description: A sunny outdoor setting with a stone table, lush green foliage, and a clear glass tank filled with water, used for a hands-on scientific demonstration involving foam blocks and buoyancy.

Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom.description: An older man with light-colored hair, initially in a dark blue shirt speaking to the camera, then in a bright orange polo shirt with gray accents, conducting a buoyancy experiment outdoors. He is identified by on-screen text as 'Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, University of New Mexico,' and audibly introduced by a clear, authoritative narrator as 'Structural geologist Karl Karlstrom from the University of New Mexico.' He speaks clearly and articulately, explaining geological concepts like erosion, rock uplift, and the dynamic nature of mountains, referring to the manipulation of 'blocks' and 'layers' in his demonstration.

raw_segments: The video opens with sweeping aerial shots of rugged, sunlit mountain ranges, showcasing layered rock formations and deep valleys under a clear blue sky, accompanied by a consistent, somewhat dramatic instrumental background music and subtle ambient sounds. The scene transitions to a close-up of Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, an older man with light-colored hair, wearing a dark blue shirt, standing outdoors with mountains in the background, speaking directly to the camera. A clear, authoritative male narrator introduces him as 'Structural geologist Karl Karlstrom from the University of New Mexico,' explaining he 'demonstrates using blocks of ice' where 'red and green ice layers represent the mountains above ground, and the white ice their roots below ground.' The setting then shifts to a sunny outdoor environment with lush green foliage, where Karlstrom, now in a bright orange polo shirt with gray accents, conducts a hands-on experiment. Visually, he places two large, cylindrical blocks of white foam into a clear glass tank filled with water, demonstrating buoyancy and displacement, adjusting them and observing how they float, creating ripples and bubbles. On-screen text identifies him as 'Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, University of New Mexico,' with the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo displayed. Audibly, Karlstrom speaks clearly and articulately, explaining that 'mountains are not static things,' and the earth's surface is 'continually being reshaped by erosion, by rivers, by glaciers.' He demonstrates, stating, 'I'll just pull one of the blocks off,' explaining how 'the overall surface has gone down,' and 'this rock, which was deep in the earth a minute ago... has come to the surface. That's called rock uplift.' He continues, 'I guess I'll erode off the pink layer too,' describing a 'continent without a major mountain belt.' The background music continues throughout his explanation.

event_blocks.summary: The Rocky Mountains' formation is explored, beginning with their geological origins in Canada due to thrust faulting and erosion, and contrasting them with the American Rockies, which are characterized by ancient granite. The narrative delves into the tectonic processes, explaining how the subducting Pacific Ocean plate, initially plunging steeply into the mantle, began to subduct at a shallower, horizontal angle approximately 70 million years ago. This shallow subduction caused compression, leading to crustal folding and uplift, forming the mountain ranges. Dr. Kirk R. Johnson likens this to a spatula under a pizza, explaining the formation of the Rockies 68 million years ago. Field geologists, including Kirk Johnson and his assistants Veronica and Ian, conduct outdoor experiments to demonstrate how granite peaks punched up through surrounding red rock layers. They use an olive-green tarp to symbolize sedimentary layers covering the granite, illustrating how erosion exposes the granite core. The process of dating the Rockies involves analyzing debris from the mountains, such as sedimentary rock, fossils, and volcanic ash beds, found in adjacent basins. Dr. Doug Nichols, from the Denver Museum of Nature & Science, uses microscopic fossilized pollen to date the uplift, explaining that specific plant species found in sediment indicate the age of the mountain formation. The uplift is estimated to have begun 65 to 70 million years ago. A catastrophic asteroid impact 65.5 million years ago, which wiped out the dinosaurs, left a distinctive pale yellow layer in the sediments near the Rockies, containing asteroid remains. This layer, sandwiched between debris eroding from the Rockies, confirms that the mountains were actively forming and eroding at the time of impact. Kirk Johnson further refines the dating by specializing in fossilized leaves.

The narrator explains its significance in forming mountains and the presence of granite, specifically how the "shallow angle trajectory of the Pacific Ocean plate explains why these mountains formed so far inland."
