raw_segments: The minute opens with a breathtaking aerial view of a snow-covered mountain ridge under a clear blue sky, showcasing rugged peaks and deep valleys, accompanied by a male narrator explaining that "The root rebounds upward" and that "The Rockies are constantly topped up from beneath." As the camera glides along, revealing expansive mountain ranges, the narrator continues, detailing how "In Canada, thrust faulting and erosion have shaped the mountains." The perspective then shifts dramatically to a rotating view of Earth from space, highlighting North and South America, before zooming into a detailed topographical view of mountainous terrain, while the narrator notes that "as we travel south, this distinctive landscape is about to change." He then specifies that "Just south of the US border in the state of Montana, a new rock appears among the Rockies: Granite," concluding that "the mountains of the American Rockies are radically different." Throughout this segment, a dramatic, orchestral score plays. The scene transitions to a serene valley with a reflective lake, surrounded by snow-dusted mountains and dense forests, under a partly cloudy sky. The audio then shifts to a female voice remarking, "I think that's an interesting thing," followed by a different male voice stating, "This one piece will make 52 layers," as the video cuts to a series of promotional frames. These frames include a woman applying makeup with the text 'ALL THE GREATEST ENTERTAINMENT', the man holding a pastry with 'QUALITY DOCUMENTARIES AND TV SERIES', a smartphone displaying a nature scene with 'OR THE BIG SCREEN', and a man in a brown jacket with 'NO SUBSCRIPTION REQUIRED'. A female voiceover then explicitly states, "Watch on mobile devices or the big screen. All for free. No subscription required," over an upbeat, modern jingle. The final frames show a diver exploring an underwater seabed with rocky formations and a mist-covered mountain and lake, with the text 'Uncharted Canada' appearing in the corner, suggesting a documentary or exploration theme, as the promotional jingle continues.

The minute opens with an uplifting, slightly dramatic orchestral score accompanying a sweeping aerial view of a rugged, snow-dusted mountain range, showcasing jagged peaks, deep valleys, expansive glaciers, and rocky outcrops. A male narrator begins, stating, "On our journey south along the Rocky Mountains, we're entering a very different landscape." The scene transitions to a stunning, rotating view of Earth from space, displaying North and South America with swirling clouds against the blackness. The perspective then shifts back to Earth, zooming into a detailed topographical view of a mountainous region with snow-capped summits, dense forests, and deep canyons. The narrator explains, "From Montana southward, the Rockies are made from ancient granite, 1.7 billion years old," adding that "Granite makes up much of the deepest part of the continental crust," which geologists call "the basement." The camera pans across various mountain ranges, including a prominent granite peak and a forested ridge bathed in golden sunlight, suggesting changing light. The narrator continues, contrasting that "The Canadian Rockies are built from sedimentary rocks piled up on top of the continental foundations," and then poses a question, "So why does granite suddenly appear here in the American Rockies?" The sequence concludes with a wide, panoramic shot of a lush, green mountain range under a bright blue sky, with distant snow-covered peaks, as the narrator presents "an even greater puzzle": "Mountains usually form close to plate boundaries. But the Southern Rockies sit a long way from the plate margin." The dramatic background music continues throughout.

The video opens with a breathtaking aerial view of Earth from space, showcasing North and South America against a starry background, accompanied by a cinematic, slightly ominous background score. As the camera transitions into a detailed topographical zoom into mountainous terrain, a narrator explains, "The Front Range in Colorado is a thousand miles from where the Pacific and North American plates actually meet." The scene then dives into a 3D animated cross-section of Earth's crust, illustrating tectonic plate movement with a continental plate sliding over a molten core, highlighted by arrows indicating direction and heat flow. The narrator continues, "Geologists have come up with an explanation. They believe that the subducting Pacific Ocean plate is responsible." A deep rumbling sound underscores the animation as the narrator details how "Ocean crust had been pushed deep into the mantle beneath North America for a hundred million years, when something unusual happened." The sequence alternates between the animated tectonic model and real footage of rugged, snow-dusted mountain peaks, emphasizing the geological forces at play. The music becomes more pronounced as the narrator explains, "70 million years ago, the Pacific Ocean plate started to subduct at a shallower angle. Instead of plummeting steeply, it sliced beneath North America horizontally." The animation continues to show the formation of mountain ranges through crustal folding and uplift, with the surface layer undulating into peaks, seamlessly blending scientific explanation with natural beauty, using dynamic camera movements and vivid color contrasts. The narrator concludes, "This change had dramatic consequences."

The video begins with a 3D animated cross-section of Earth's crust, illustrating tectonic plate movement. A gray plate moves horizontally over a molten orange-red mantle, with arrows indicating direction and upward force, causing the overlying brown crust to buckle and form mountain-like ridges. Dr. Kirk R. Johnson's voice explains that the Pacific plate "didn't go deep down, it went in shallow like a spatula under a pizza," leading to the formation of the Rocky Mountains 68 million years ago. The animation transitions to a real-world outdoor setting where Dr. Kirk R. Johnson, wearing glasses and a light gray collared shirt, stands in front of layered red rock formations, speaking directly to the camera, with the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo appearing in the lower third. The scene cuts back to the animation, now showing vertical fractures in the crust as the plate continues to move, causing the surface to uplift and form a wavy topography. The animation then transitions to a real mountain landscape with snow-capped peaks and glaciers, overlaid with the animated crust for visual comparison, as a narrator's voice explains that "for millions of years, the ocean plate scraped along the underside of North America," creating friction that broke up the basement granite and punched it upward, accompanied by grinding and a deep, resonant hum. The video then shifts to Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, an older man with light-colored hair wearing a dark blue polo shirt, standing outdoors in a mountainous area with a valley in the background. The narrator introduces him as "Structural geologist Karl Karlstrom," who then speaks to the camera, using his hands to demonstrate a concept, holding a small object (possibly a rock or mineral sample) in his palm and gesturing with his other hand. He explains, "Because it was at a shallower angle beneath North America, it was scraping along the base of North America. When that happens, it puts the plate under compression like this," as the camera zooms in on his hands, emphasizing his precise finger movements, including a close-up of his ring finger with a gold band, while the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo reappears.

The video opens with Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, an older man with light-colored hair wearing a dark blue polo shirt, standing outdoors in a mountainous area with a valley visible in the background. He speaks directly to the camera, using expressive hand gestures to explain how a plate "scraped along the bottom so it squeezes, pushes up the mountains, and it transferred this mountain building from the edge to great distance from the plate margin." Upbeat, slightly dramatic instrumental music begins as the scene transitions to sweeping panoramic views of rugged mountain ranges under a partly cloudy sky, showcasing layered rock formations and deep valleys. A narrator explains that "The shallow angle trajectory of the Pacific Ocean plate explains why these mountains formed so far inland. And it also explains the presence of granite." This is followed by a 3D animated cross-section illustrating tectonic plate movement, where a gray plate moves over a molten orange-red mantle, causing the overlying crust to buckle and form mountain ridges, with arrows indicating direction and force. The narrator continues, describing how "Thrust up through layers of sedimentary rock, the broken granite became the Rocky Mountains of the South." The animation then shows vertical fractures and uplift. The video cuts to real footage of striking red rock formations, including balanced rocks and eroded cliffs, under a bright blue sky, as the narrator identifies them as "Red Rocks Park, Denver, Colorado," a "landscape forged by granite uplift." The final scene shows two individuals lying prone on a red rock surface, with a third man in a light green shirt kneeling beside them, appearing to conduct a hands-on demonstration or measurement. The narrator identifies this man as "Kirk Johnson," demonstrating "how these granite peaks punched up through the layers of red rock around them." Kirk Johnson then speaks, introducing his "assistants, Veronica, and Ian," as a vast arid landscape stretches into the distance.

The video opens on a sunny outdoor experiment setup on a red rock surface, where a man in a light green shirt kneels beside two prone individuals, one in a striped shirt and green pants, the other in a beige shirt and blue jeans. The kneeling man explains how the Rocky Mountains formed, asking viewers to imagine the prone individuals, whom he refers to as Veronica and Ian, are composed of "1.7 billion year old metamorphic rocks." Dramatic, slightly ominous background music begins as a narrator adds that before the mountains grew, layers of sedimentary rock covered the granite. Suddenly, a large olive-green tarp is thrown over Veronica and Ian, completely covering them, symbolizing these sedimentary layers. The man in the green shirt then adjusts the tarp, smoothing it, and proceeds to lift one end, revealing Ian, who is now propped up on his elbows. The man continues his explanation, detailing how "the mountain where Ian is starts to break and lift up," causing overlying sediments to erode off, exposing "Ian's granitic back" as the core of the Rocky Mountains, while Veronica remains "deeply buried beneath Denver." The scene then cuts to a different outdoor location, showing two hikers with red and blue backpacks walking along a rocky ridge in a dry, grassy landscape, as the dramatic background music continues and a narrator briefly mentions "Johnson is investigating exactly when these..."

The segment opens with a wide shot of a dry, sunlit hillside where three individuals are engaged in geological fieldwork, accompanied by a male narrator explaining that "dating the Rockies requires real detective work" and that "you get clues by digging up dirt," set against a backdrop of instrumental music. A man in a straw hat uses a pickaxe to break apart rocky soil, while two others, one in a striped shirt with a white visor and another in a light gray t-shirt and cap, kneel nearby, examining rocks and using hand tools. The camera zooms in on the man with the pickaxe, capturing the forceful impact as he chips away at the earth, then cuts to close-ups of hands carefully extracting and inspecting rock fragments. Dr. Kirk R. Johnson, a man with short brown hair and glasses, then speaks directly to the camera, explaining that "to understand mountains, you don't look at the mountains... you look at the debris from those mountains," likening the basin adjacent to the mountain range to a "garbage can" containing "sedimentary rock and fossils, volcanic ash beds, layers of lava" that allows them to "reconstruct the chronology of the uplifting mountain range." The scene returns to the fieldwork under bright sunlight, with the rocky, layered formations dominating the frame, as the narrator concludes that "this debris forms layers of sediment 10,000 feet thick beneath the city of Denver," while a sweeping panoramic shot reveals a distant city skyline, emphasizing the proximity of urban development to the natural terrain, all underscored by the instrumental music.

The video opens with an atmospheric background music as a sweeping aerial view showcases a rugged mountain range with snow-capped peaks and deep valleys, partially shrouded in clouds, establishing a grand geological context. A male narrator explains that "in their mission to date these massive peaks, Johnson and his colleagues search for the tiniest clues, like fossilized leaves and pollen." The scene transitions to a sunlit, arid hillside where a Field Geologist in a Straw Hat, wearing a gray shirt and khaki pants, actively uses a pickaxe to break apart rocky soil, demonstrating field excavation. He then holds up a rock fragment, revealing its layered texture and embedded fossils, emphasizing geological discovery. The setting shifts to a futuristic, dimly lit tunnel with blue and purple lighting, leading into a museum or lab environment. Inside, Dr. Doug Nichols, an older man with white hair and a light blue shirt, identified by a lower-third graphic from the Denver Museum of Nature & Science, speaks directly to the camera. The narrator introduces him, stating that "these microscopic grains of pollen provide a valuable lead." Dr. Nichols, in a calm, explanatory voice, elaborates, "Pollen can be used to date the uplift of mountains because plants that were living at the time the mountains were coming up were shedding their pollen, which was becoming incorporated into sediment, which was being eroded from those same mountains." He continues, "And I'm looking for the particular species of pollen that can tell me the age of the sample." The background includes a microscope and a computer screen displaying magnified fossil-like structures. The video then shows close-ups of Dr. Nichols examining a slide under the microscope, with the computer screen displaying a blue field of microscopic fossils, including a prominent circular specimen with radial patterns. His hands are shown handling a red-framed slide holder, placing a slide under the microscope, and adjusting the lens. The narrator adds that "each sample of sediment can contain thousands of fossilized pollen particles." The final frames focus on the computer screen, where a yellow box labeled 'SPOT' highlights a specific fossil, indicating detailed scientific analysis, as the narrator begins to state, "Nichols examines a single..." The 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo appears in the lower third during Dr. Nichols’ interview, reinforcing the program’s branding.

The segment opens with a close-up of a computer screen displaying a magnified, blue-tinted microscopic image of fossilized structures, with a yellow box labeled 'SPOT' highlighting a circular specimen with radial patterns. A calm, authoritative male narrator explains that "Every plant genus has distinctive shaped pollen," and by identifying plant species and their age, "Nichols can start to date the exact uplift of Colorado's Rockies." The scene transitions to Dr. Doug Nichols, an older man with white hair and a white beard, wearing a light blue shirt, seated in a laboratory. He is shown using a microscope, adjusting its objective lens labeled '40x/0.95', and interacting with the computer screen displaying the same fossilized images. Speaking directly to the camera with a clear, articulate voice, Dr. Nichols states, "We pretty well understand that the uplift began 65 to 70 million years ago because we find distinctive kinds of pollen produced by plants that only lived 65 to 70 million years ago." As he speaks, sweeping panoramic views of The Rockies are shown, showcasing rugged mountain ranges, dense forests, and valleys under a partly cloudy sky, with sunlight casting dynamic shadows, emphasizing their vastness and geological scale. A deep, resonant hum begins to build tension in the background. The narrator then adds that "Sometime during this 5 million year period, Colorado's Rockies began to rise." The hum intensifies as the video transitions to a dramatic animated sequence: a mountain peak is shown from Earth's perspective, then the view zooms out into space, revealing Earth rotating against a starry backdrop. The animation continues with a close-up of a rocky asteroid, overlaid with scientific equations, colliding with Earth, creating a fiery explosion and debris, symbolizing a catastrophic geological event. The narrator's voice declares, "But geologists can narrow this window even further, thanks to one of the most catastrophic events in history," which is underscored by a loud whooshing and a sustained, rumbling explosion sound accompanying the asteroid impact. The final frame is a blurred, abstract image of the explosion, as the narrator states, "65.5 million..." before the audio cuts off.

The minute opens with a dramatic animated sequence, accompanied by intense, low-frequency music and impact sound effects, depicting a catastrophic asteroid impact on Earth, first from a coastal perspective with a fiery explosion and mushroom cloud, then from space with the planet engulfed in flames and debris. Narration explains that "An asteroid smashed into the Earth," wiping out the dinosaurs and leaving "a distinctive line in the sediments by Colorado's Rocky Mountains." The scene transitions to a desolate, post-impact landscape with dead trees and a smoldering horizon, followed by a close-up of a large, lifeless dinosaur, emphasizing extinction. The narrator continues, stating "This pale yellow layer in the rock contains the remains of the huge asteroid." The narrative then shifts to a real-world, sun-drenched arid landscape, where aerial shots reveal a dry, grassy hillside with sparse vegetation, accompanied by the sound of wind. The camera zooms in to show two field geologists— a woman in a white visor and striped shirt, and a man in a cap and light gray shirt— actively excavating and examining rock fragments. The man holds up a broken rock piece, revealing a fossilized structure with a distinct circular pattern. The narration explains that because the debris is "sandwiched between layers of debris that eroded off the Rockies," it indicates the Rockies were "up and eroding at the time of impact, 65.5 million years ago." The scene cuts to Kirk Johnson, a man in a straw hat, carefully breaking apart rock with a pickaxe, the distinct sound of which is heard. The narrator identifies him, stating "Kirk Johnson specializes in dating fossilized leaves," using them "to date the Rockies' birth even more precisely." The final shot transitions to an underwater seabed, showing a vibrant, textured ocean floor with coral-like formations in turquoise water, as the narration begins to say "Geologists know that..." before the segment ends.

event_blocks.summary: An aerial view of a vibrant underwater seabed transitions to a sweeping shot of a mist-covered mountain and lake, accompanied by a dramatic orchestral score, as a narrator explains that 69 million years ago, Colorado was covered by a shallow sea. In a dimly lit laboratory, a Scientist examines magnified fossil-like structures, including circular, pinkish-red specimens, on a computer screen displaying a "SPOT" box. The narrator notes that just a million years later, plants existed, evidenced by Johnson's fossil leaves. On an arid hillside, a Field Geologist in a straw hat, a Female Field Assistant, and a Male Field Assistant excavate rocks; a close-up shows the Male Field Assistant holding a fossilized rock specimen with a concentric structure. As the music intensifies, the narrator explains that Johnson combines his findings with Nickel's pollen evidence to establish a million-year window for the emergence of the Colorado Rockies. A panoramic view of The Rockies showcases their grandeur. Dr. Kirk R. Johnson, speaking from the arid hillside, reiterates that 69 million years ago, Colorado was covered by a shallow sea, and by 68 million years ago, the Rocky Mountains had emerged, with the precise timing within that million-year period still under investigation. The segment concludes with an aerial view of a snow-capped mountain range, including Half Dome, as the music swells and fades.

Scientist.description: An older man with white hair, wearing a light blue shirt, seated at a desk in a laboratory, using a microscope and interacting with a computer displaying scientific data.

Fossilized Rock Specimen.description: A broken piece of sedimentary rock held by the Male Field Assistant, revealing a circular, concentric fossil structure with a central dark core, likely a preserved ancient organism such as a coral or shell, embedded in light brown matrix.

Computer Screen Display.description: A monitor showing a blue background with magnified, fossil-like structures, including a prominent circular specimen with radial patterns, with a yellow box labeled 'SPOT' highlighting a specific area for detailed examination.

Earth from Space.description: A rotating view of Earth showing North and South America, with visible cloud patterns and landmasses, set against a starry background, transitioning to a detailed topographical view of mountainous terrain.

Field Geologist in Straw Hat.description: A man wearing a straw hat, gray short-sleeved shirt, and khaki pants, actively using a pickaxe to break apart rocky soil on a sunlit hillside, demonstrating physical excavation work.

Female Field Assistant.description: A woman wearing a white visor, striped short-sleeved shirt, and gray pants, kneeling on the ground and using a small hammer to chip at rocks, focused on detailed geological sampling.

Male Field Assistant.description: A man in a light gray t-shirt and cap, kneeling beside the female assistant, using a hand tool to examine rock fragments, engaged in close-up geological analysis.

Mist-Covered Mountain and Lake.description: A mountain range partially obscured by low-hanging clouds, with a calm lake in the foreground and dense evergreen forests along the shoreline, under an overcast sky, transitioning into a time-lapse with shifting light and moving clouds.

The Rockies.description: A vast mountain landscape featuring rugged peaks, deep valleys, and layered rock formations, captured through panoramic and aerial shots under a partly cloudy sky, emphasizing geological scale and natural beauty. The narrator states they 'must have emerged' during a 'million-year window,' and Dr. Kirk R. Johnson later explains that they 'were up' 68 million years ago, within a 1-million-year period.

Dr. Kirk R. Johnson.description: A man with short brown hair and glasses, wearing a light gray collared shirt, standing outdoors in a rocky, arid landscape with layered red rock formations and sparse vegetation, speaking directly to the camera. He explains in a clear, explanatory voice that 69 million years ago Colorado was a shallow sea, and 68 million years ago the Rocky Mountains had emerged, noting the precise timing within that million-year period is still under investigation.

Arid Hillside Landscape.description: A dry, sun-baked hillside covered in sparse, golden-brown grass and low shrubs, with visible erosion patterns and exposed soil, serving as the fieldwork site for the geologists.

Underwater Seabed.description: A rocky ocean floor with a turquoise hue, illuminated by light filtering through water, transitioning from shallow, sunlit areas to deeper, darker regions, suggesting a deep-sea environment.

raw_segments: The segment opens with an aerial view of a vibrant underwater seabed, followed by a sweeping aerial shot of a mountainous landscape with a deep blue lake, set to an orchestral, slightly dramatic background score. A narrator begins, explaining that "69 million years ago, Colorado was covered by a shallow sea." The scene transitions to a dimly lit laboratory where a Scientist uses a microscope and computer, which displays magnified fossil-like structures, including circular, pinkish-red specimens highlighted by a 'SPOT' box. The narrator continues, stating that "Johnson's fossil leaves show that just a million years later, there were plants here." The video then shows an arid hillside where a Field Geologist, a Female Field Assistant, and a Male Field Assistant are actively excavating rocks, with a close-up revealing the Male Field Assistant holding a fossilized rock specimen with a concentric structure. The background music becomes more intense and driving as the narrator explains, "Johnson combines his findings with Nickel's pollen evidence to come up with a million-year window during which the Colorado Rockies must have emerged." A panoramic view of The Rockies, with rugged peaks and dense forests, is shown, emphasizing their grandeur. Dr. Kirk R. Johnson then appears, speaking directly to the camera from the arid hillside, reiterating in his clear, explanatory voice that "69 million years ago, Colorado was covered by a shallow sea," and "68 million years ago, the Rocky Mountains were up." He elaborates that the exact timing of their emergence within that one-million-year period is still being investigated. The segment concludes with an aerial view of a snow-capped mountain range, including Half Dome, as the background music swells and then fades.

event_blocks.summary: Sweeping aerial and panoramic views of the American Rockies, which arrived 68 million years ago, showcase snow-dusted granite peaks, forested slopes, and deep valleys under a dynamic sky, accompanied by gentle, ambient background music. As the camera pans across layered rock formations, including striking red sandstone cliffs, the landscape evolves once again while traveling south. A dramatic shift to a satellite-style topographical view of a volcanic region precedes intense, fiery close-ups of molten lava erupting, underscored by a dramatic rumbling sound effect. Volcanoes appeared among the peaks 35 million years ago, triggered by changes deep beneath America, marking a new explosive era. The background music intensifies and shifts to a grander, more adventurous tone as the footage returns to high-altitude mountain scenes with snow patches and expansive tree-covered hills. The journey covers 2,000 miles through the Rockies and forward through their 200 million year history.

Satellite Topographical View.description: A high-altitude, 3D-rendered view of a desert-like region with volcanic cones, river valleys, and green vegetation, resembling a digital Earth model with detailed terrain features.

The Rockies.description: A vast mountain landscape featuring rugged peaks, deep valleys, and layered rock formations, captured through panoramic and aerial shots under a partly cloudy sky, emphasizing geological scale and natural beauty. The narrator explicitly identifies these as 'the American Rockies,' stating they arrived 68 million years ago, and later refers to traveling through them, hinting at their 200-million-year history.

Volcanic Eruption Footage.description: Intense, fiery close-ups of molten lava erupting with bright orange and red flows and billowing smoke, indicating active volcanic activity and geological transformation. This visual is accompanied by a dramatic rumbling sound effect and the narrator's explanation that 'volcanoes appeared among the peaks' 35 million years ago, triggered by 'changes deep beneath America,' marking 'a new explosive era'.

Layered Rock Formations.description: Prominent geological features with distinct horizontal strata in shades of brown, gray, and orange, showing signs of erosion and mineral variation, visible in real footage of red rock landscapes with balanced rocks and eroded cliffs.

Earth from Space.description: A rotating view of Earth showing North and South America, with visible cloud patterns and landmasses, set against a starry background, transitioning to a detailed topographical view of mountainous terrain.

Asteroids and Scientific Equations.description: Floating rocky asteroids in space, overlaid with equations such as 'Gravitational potential energy' and 'Tangential acceleration', suggesting a scientific explanation of geological formation.

raw_segments: The segment opens with sweeping aerial and panoramic views of rugged mountain landscapes, identified by the narrator as "the American Rockies," which he states "had arrived" 68 million years ago. Gentle, ambient background music accompanies these visuals, showcasing snow-dusted granite peaks, forested slopes, and deep valleys under a dynamic sky. The camera pans across layered rock formations, including striking red sandstone cliffs, as the narrator explains that the landscape "evolves once again" as they continue south through the Rockies. A dramatic shift occurs with a satellite-style topographical view of a volcanic region, followed by intense, fiery close-ups of molten lava erupting. This volcanic activity is underscored by a dramatic rumbling sound effect and the narrator's explanation that "volcanoes appeared among the peaks" 35 million years ago, triggered by "changes deep beneath America," marking "a new explosive era." The background music intensifies and then shifts to a grander, more adventurous tone as the footage returns to high-altitude mountain scenes with snow patches and expansive tree-covered hills. The narrator concludes by mentioning traveling "2,000 miles through the Rockies" and "forward through their 200 million" year history.

event_blocks.summary: A 3D satellite topographical view of a vast, arid landscape with rugged mountains and volcanic cones transitions to volcanic eruption footage, showing molten lava bursting from a crater and spewing fragments into the air. A tectonic plate animation overlay then illustrates the formation of the Rockies, depicting a dark brown tectonic plate moving over a glowing orange-red mantle, causing fractures and upward buckling that form mountain ridges, as a narrator explains that 70 million years ago, the subducting Pacific Ocean plate punched up granite blocks. The animation further shows the Pacific Ocean plate peeling away 35 million years later, allowing warm mantle to contact the underside of the North American plate beneath the Southern Rocky Mountains, leading to the formation of smooth, beige mountainous terrain.

Tectonic Plate Animation Overlay.description: A 3D animated cross-section of Earth's crust showing a horizontal gray plate moving over a molten orange-red mantle, causing the overlying brown crust to buckle and form mountain ridges, with arrows indicating movement and upward force, which the narrator explains as the 'subducting Pacific Ocean plate' forming the Rockies and later peeling away, bringing 'warm mantle' into contact with the 'North American plate' to form the Southern Rocky Mountains.

Satellite Topographical View.description: A high-altitude, 3D-rendered view of a desert-like region with volcanic cones, river valleys, and green vegetation, resembling a digital Earth model with detailed terrain features, shown under a clear blue sky, which the narrator identifies as the region of Albuquerque, New Mexico, and the evolving Rockies.

Volcanic Eruption Footage.description: Intense, fiery close-ups of molten lava erupting, with bright orange and red flows and billowing smoke, visually representing the 'explosive phase' of geological activity described by the narrator, set against dramatic orchestral music.

raw_segments: The segment commences with a sweeping 3D satellite topographical view of a vast, arid landscape, featuring rugged mountain ranges, deep valleys, and scattered volcanic cones rendered in earthy greens, browns, and ochres under a clear blue sky, while a narrator announces, "We reach Albuquerque, New Mexico." Dramatic orchestral music underscores the narrator's explanation that a "dramatic change deep beneath America triggered the most explosive phase in the evolution of the Rockies." This narrative is visually punctuated by an abrupt transition to intense, fiery close-ups of a volcanic eruption, with molten lava bursting violently from a dark crater, spewing glowing orange and yellow fragments into the air amidst thick smoke. The scene then shifts to a simplified 3D animated cross-section of Earth’s crust, depicting a dark brown tectonic plate moving horizontally over a glowing orange-red mantle, with visible fractures and upward buckling forming mountain ridges. The narrator details, "70 million years ago, the subducting Pacific Ocean plate punched up huge blocks of basement granite, forming the Rockies of New Mexico." As the animation progresses, the surface layer deforms into undulating peaks and valleys, illustrating mountain range formation through tectonic uplift, with the molten mantle continuously pushing upward. The narrator further explains, "35 million years later, the Pacific Ocean plate began to peel away from the underside of the North American continent," and "As the ocean plate sunk deeper, warm mantle came into contact with the underside of the North American plate, right underneath parts of the Southern Rocky Mountains." The final frames focus on the newly formed, smooth, beige mountainous terrain, emphasizing the geological transformation from subterranean forces to surface topography, all under a consistent light blue sky, with the dramatic orchestral score continuing throughout.

event_blocks.summary: The powerful whoosh and roar of a hot air balloon's burner system ignites, producing a bright, intense flame that illuminates the interior of the multi-patterned Balloon Envelope, featuring yellow, red, blue, and green panels. A narrator explains the principle of buoyancy, stating, "Just as the burner on a hot air balloon makes the air less dense, causing the balloon to float upward through the cooler air around it." The Hot Air Balloon ascends, transitioning to an expansive Aerial Landscape view showing it drifting over roads, buildings, and a river. The narrator draws an analogy: "So warm, buoyant mantle rose up through the cooler, denser mantle beneath the mountains," and introduces Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, suggesting, "Karl Karlstrom thinks that this caused the Rockies to grow even taller." Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, identified under the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo, speaks from the Balloon Basket, elaborating, "Well, let's think about uplift of mountains in the context of this analogy of a hot air balloon. We're floating high because we're more buoyant than the air around us." He continues, "And if we want to go up, actually get still higher, uh, we'll just turn on the burner and uh, fire it up and then the air becomes lighter within the balloon, causing a dynamic or an active uplift." The video alternates between close-ups of Prof. Karlstrom and a Man in Gray Shirt and Cap looking out, and wide aerial shots of the Hot Air Balloon against a clear blue sky, floating serenely over a valley with distant mountains, with the 'NAKED SCIENCE' branding visible.

Man in Gray Shirt and Cap.description: A man wearing a gray short-sleeved shirt with a logo on the chest and a beige cap, standing in the hot air balloon basket next to Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, holding a rope with a yellow glove, looking out at the scenery with a calm expression.

Balloon Basket.description: A metal-framed basket with visible chains and support structures, housing the passengers and burner system, shown from within and from an aerial perspective as the balloon ascends.

Balloon Envelope.description: The multi-colored fabric of the hot air balloon, featuring bold geometric patterns in yellow, red, blue, and green, shown inflating and expanding as it fills with hot air, with the interior illuminated by the burner's flame.

Hot Air Balloon.description: A large, colorful hot air balloon with a vibrant pattern of yellow, red, blue, and green panels, shown inflating with a powerful flame from the burner, ascending into the sky with a distinct whooshing sound, and drifting over a valley with mountains in the distance, viewed from both inside and above, used by Prof. Karlstrom as an analogy for geological uplift.

Burner System.description: A metallic, industrial-looking burner with a ribbed nozzle, mounted on a metal frame, emitting a powerful, bright orange flame with a distinct whooshing and roaring sound to heat the balloon's envelope, shown in close-up and from within the balloon's interior, and mentioned by Prof. Karlstrom as he describes increasing altitude.

NAKED SCIENCE.description: A recurring on-screen graphic in the lower third, featuring a blue and white design with a vertical line and the text 'NAKED SCIENCE' in bold white letters, consistently displayed during the field segment, identifying the program's branding.

Aerial Landscape.description: An expansive view from above, showing a valley with roads, buildings, a river, green fields, and distant mountains, captured during the balloon's ascent, with sunlight reflecting off the river and casting shadows across the terrain.

Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom.description: An older man with light-colored hair, wearing a red jacket with a black zipper and sunglasses hanging from the collar, standing in a hot air balloon basket, smiling and speaking with a clear, slightly academic voice, explaining the analogy between the balloon's uplift and geological processes, identified by an on-screen graphic as 'Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, University of New Mexico' under the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo.

raw_segments: The segment opens with the powerful whoosh and roar of a hot air balloon's burner igniting, producing a bright, intense flame that fills the frame with orange and yellow light, illuminating the interior of the multi-patterned envelope. A male narrator explains, "Just as the burner on a hot air balloon makes the air less dense, causing the balloon to float upward through the cooler air around it," as the colorful balloon, featuring yellow, red, blue, and green panels, inflates and ascends. The camera shifts to an expansive aerial perspective, showing the balloon drifting over a landscape of roads, buildings, and a river, while the narrator continues, drawing an analogy: "So warm, buoyant mantle rose up through the cooler, denser mantle beneath the mountains." He then introduces Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, stating, "Karl Karlstrom thinks that this caused the Rockies to grow even taller." The scene cuts to Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, identified by an on-screen graphic as 'Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, University of New Mexico' under the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo, speaking from the basket with a clear, slightly academic voice. He elaborates, "Well, let's think about uplift of mountains in the context of this analogy of a hot air balloon. We're floating high because we're more buoyant than the air around us." As he speaks, the burner sound is heard again, and he continues, "And if we want to go up, actually get still higher, uh, we'll just turn on the burner and uh, fire it up and then the air becomes lighter within the balloon, causing a dynamic or an active uplift." The video alternates between close-ups of Prof. Karlstrom and another man in a gray shirt and cap looking out, and wide aerial shots of the vibrant balloon against a clear blue sky, floating serenely over a valley with distant mountains, all while the 'NAKED SCIENCE' branding remains visible.

event_blocks.summary: A man in a gray shirt and cap, alongside Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, observes the landscape from a hot air balloon basket as a narrator explains geological forces pushing up mountains, transitioning to a 3D animation of a desert terrain being uplifted into rugged peaks. The animation illustrates crustal stretching and thinning due to upwelling mantle, forming the Rio Grande Rift, with the narrator detailing crustal thickness variations. Real-world footage of an arid canyon landscape follows, with the narrator explaining that thin crust under immense pressure gave way, culminating in dramatic footage of a volcanic eruption where red-hot magma blasted onto the New Mexico landscape. Intense, fiery close-ups of molten lava erupting are shown, with a narrator stating volcanoes punctured the Rio Grande Rift, transitioning to a satellite topographical view and aerial shots of volcanic cones and rugged mountains. Scientists believe hot magma has surged from the mantle many times over 35 million years, leading to renewed volcanism, and the narrator emphasizes this is not ancient history, citing an eruption 1500 years ago that flooded New Mexico with lava. The segment questions if hot magma is still ballooning beneath the surface and if the Rockies of New Mexico are still rising, ending with a woman holding a transparent sphere. The scene then shifts to Prof. Laura J. Crossey, a geologist, assembling a water filtration system in an arid desert landscape near hot springs, explaining that fluids reach the surface due to tectonic extension, providing a window into the deep world and mantle. Close-ups show water being filtered, with bubbling sounds as gases under pressure dissolve and escape. Analyzing these gas bubbles reveals carbon dioxide and helium 3, highlighting the scientific effort in the harsh environment.

Tectonic Plate Animation Overlay.description: A 3D animated cross-section of Earth's crust, initially showing a smooth surface developing into rugged mountains due to uplift, then transitioning to layered rock strata compressed and folded by red arrows, simulating tectonic forces. This animation illustrates the upwelling mantle stretching the crust, causing the continent to pull apart, thinning and fracturing the crust to form an immense crevice, the Rio Grande Rift, with the narrator detailing crust thickness variations.

Man in Gray Shirt and Cap.description: A man wearing a gray short-sleeved shirt with a logo on the chest and a beige cap, standing in the hot air balloon basket next to Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, holding a rope with a yellow glove, looking out at the scenery with a calm expression.

3D Animated Prehistoric Marine Life.description: Not visible in this segment; replaced by a 3D animated cross-section of tectonic plates.

Satellite Topographical View.description: A high-altitude, 3D-rendered view of a desert-like region with volcanic cones, river valleys, and green vegetation, resembling a digital Earth model with detailed terrain features, shown under a clear blue sky, described by the narrator as where 'volcanoes punctured the Rio Grande Rift'.

Volcanic Eruption Footage.description: Intense, fiery close-ups of molten lava erupting, with bright orange and red flows and billowing smoke, indicating active volcanic activity and geological transformation, explicitly linked by narration to 'volcanoes puncturing the Rio Grande Rift' and 'renewed volcanism' from surging magma.

Arid Hillside Landscape.description: A vast, arid canyon landscape with layered red rock formations, sparse vegetation, and distant mesas under a hazy sky, across which the camera pans, revealing deep canyons and eroded cliffs. This landscape is presented as the dramatic result of the thin crust giving way under immense pressure from rising heat in the mantle, as described by the narrator.

Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom.description: An older man with light-colored hair, wearing a red jacket with a black zipper and sunglasses hanging from the collar, standing in a hot air balloon basket, smiling and speaking, with an on-screen graphic identifying him as 'Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, University of New Mexico' under the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo.

Lava Flow.description: An expansive aerial shot of a river of molten lava flowing across a dark landscape at dusk, glowing bright orange and red, winding through the terrain like a fiery ribbon, which the narrator describes as the result of an eruption 1500 years ago that 'flooded New Mexico with a river of molten lava 44 miles long'.

Woman holding transparent sphere.description: A woman with brown hair, wearing a light-colored floral shirt, holding a large, clear, spherical object—possibly a lens or scientific instrument—up to the camera against a bright, cloudy sky, smiling as she presents it.

Mist-Covered Mountain and Lake.description: A mountain range partially obscured by low-hanging clouds, with a calm lake in the foreground and dense evergreen forests along the shoreline, under an overcast sky, transitioning into a time-lapse with shifting light and moving clouds, shown as the narrator discusses 'renewed volcanism in the Rio Grande Rift'.

The Rockies.description: A vast mountain landscape featuring rugged peaks, deep valleys, and layered rock formations, captured through panoramic and aerial shots under a partly cloudy sky, emphasizing geological scale and natural beauty, with the narrator questioning if 'the Rockies of New Mexico' are 'still on the rise'.

Man in dark blue shirt.description: An older man with short brown hair, wearing a dark blue short-sleeved shirt, beige pants, and sunglasses, crouching and later sitting beside a water pool, assisting in assembling a water filtration system with clear tubing and a glass apparatus. He is seen observing the process closely and interacting with the equipment.

Prof. Laura J. Crossey.description: A woman with long dark hair, wearing a floral-patterned shirt with pink and green hues, beige shorts, and black sandals, speaking directly to the camera in a desert landscape. She is identified by an on-screen graphic as 'Prof. Laura J. Crossey, University of New Mexico' under the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo, and her clear, explanatory female voice is heard describing the geological significance of the fault and the fluids finding their way to the surface.

Arid desert landscape.description: A dry, sunlit environment with reddish-brown soil, sparse green shrubs, and eroded hills under a bright blue sky with scattered clouds. The terrain features weathered logs forming a natural barrier around a shallow water pool, emphasizing the harsh conditions in which the water filtration is being tested.

NAKED SCIENCE.description: A recurring on-screen graphic in the lower third, featuring a blue and white design with a vertical line and the text 'NAKED SCIENCE' in bold white letters, consistently displayed during the field segment, identifying the program's branding.

Water filtration system.description: A portable setup consisting of a transparent funnel, clear plastic tubing, and a small glass container with a filter, used to draw and purify water from a shallow, muddy pool in a desert environment. The system is assembled and operated by the man and woman, with close-ups showing water flowing through the components and bubbles forming during filtration.

raw_segments: The minute opens with a close-up of a man in a gray shirt and cap alongside Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom in a hot air balloon basket, observing the landscape below, as a narrator explains how geological forces are "actually pushing up the mountains." Dramatic, suspenseful music underscores the scene as it transitions to a 3D animated model of a desert-like terrain, illustrating geological uplift from a smooth, undulating surface to rugged, mountainous peaks and valleys. The narrator continues, stating that "it's not just the mountains that were affected," but that "the upwelling mantle also stretched the crust above." This is visually reinforced by a cross-sectional animation of layered rock strata, where horizontal lines are compressed and folded by opposing red arrows, simulating tectonic forces. The narrator describes this as a "sandbox simulation" demonstrating the stretching process, explaining that "as the continent pulled apart, the crust thinned and fractured," forming "an immense crevice, called the Rio Grande Rift," which "opened up in the heart of the Rockies." The animation continues to show crustal thinning as the narrator details how the crust is over 30 miles thick in parts of the southern Rockies but "just 17 miles thick" beneath the rift. The scene then shifts to real-world footage of a vast, arid canyon landscape with layered red rock formations, sparse vegetation, and distant mesas under a hazy sky, as the narrator declares, "This thin crust triggered something dramatic." The camera pans across the terrain, revealing deep canyons and eroded cliffs, while the narrator explains that "under immense pressure from rising heat in the mantle, it gave way." The segment culminates in a dramatic close-up of a volcanic eruption, with bright orange lava and billowing smoke, emphasizing the power of geological forces, as the narrator concludes that "red hot magma blasted out onto the New Mexico landscape," ending with a fiery, intense view of molten rock accompanied by powerful sound effects.

The segment opens with intense, fiery close-ups of molten lava erupting, glowing bright orange and red against a dark, smoky backdrop, all set to a dramatic, driving instrumental score. A male narrator begins, stating, "And volcanoes punctured the Rio Grande Rift," as the scene transitions to a high-altitude, 3D-rendered satellite topographical view of a desert-like region, showcasing volcanic cones, river valleys, and patches of green vegetation under a clear blue sky. The camera then moves to a sweeping aerial view of rugged mountainous terrain with layered rock formations and deep valleys, while the narrator explains, "Scientists believe that hot magma has surged up from the mantle many times over the past 35 million years." This transitions into a dramatic time-lapse of a mist-covered mountain range under shifting clouds, as the narrator adds, "These surges led to renewed volcanism in the Rio Grande Rift." The footage cuts to a close-up of glowing lava beneath dark, cracked rock, with the narrator emphasizing, "But this is not ancient history." An expansive aerial shot then reveals a river of molten lava flowing across a dark landscape at dusk, as the narrator details, "The last eruption, just 1500 years ago, flooded New Mexico with a river of molten lava 44 miles long." The scene shifts to a sunlit, rocky mountain ridge with lush green forests below, revealing a panoramic view of distant valleys and hills, prompting the narrator to ask, "Could such recent eruptions mean that hot magma is ballooning beneath Earth's surface today?" The segment concludes with a woman in a floral shirt holding a transparent, spherical object against a bright sky, smiling as she presents it to the camera, while the narrator poses the final question, "Could the Rockies of New Mexico still be on the rise?" The suspenseful instrumental music continues throughout.

The minute opens with a close-up of a hand carefully placing a transparent funnel into a shallow, muddy water pool nestled between weathered logs in an arid desert landscape, as a male narrator introduces "Laura Crossey, a geologist at the University of New Mexico," who is "on a mission to find out." Subtle background music accompanies the scene as two individuals, a man in a dark blue shirt and sunglasses and a woman in a floral shirt (Prof. Laura J. Crossey), are seen crouched, assembling a water filtration system with clear tubing and a small glass apparatus. The narrator explains that Crossey, working with Carl Carlstrom, is focusing on a fault where hot springs bubble up, providing clues from gases in the water. The camera pans across the reddish-brown desert terrain before focusing on Prof. Laura J. Crossey, identified by an on-screen graphic, who speaks directly to the camera in a clear, explanatory female voice, detailing how "this point along this fault is a place where these fluids are finding their way to the surface because of the extension, the tectonic activity. We're able to come here and have a window into that deep, deep world all the way to the mantle." The video returns to close-ups of the filtration setup, showing water being drawn through the funnel and tubing into a glass container, with distinct bubbling sounds accompanying the visual of bubbles forming as it passes through a filter. The narrator elaborates that beneath Earth's surface, gases under immense pressure dissolve in water and escape as bubbles when they reach the surface. The man and woman are then seen sitting on the logs, observing the system intently, with Prof. Crossey holding the glass container. The narrator concludes by mentioning that analyzing these gas bubbles allows Crossey to discover carbon dioxide and traces of helium 3, as the final shot shows the water pool with the filtration system in place, emphasizing the scientific effort in the harsh, arid environment.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins with a close-up of eroded rock, as the narrator explains the presence of "helium 3" and mantle byproducts indicate melting and movement within the Earth's mantle, drawing a parallel to a rising hot air balloon. This transitions to a silhouette of two individuals on a reddish-brown hill at sunset. Inside a Hot Air Balloon, the Burner System ignites with a whooshing sound, inflating the colorful Balloon Envelope as Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, in a red jacket, and another passenger observe from the Balloon Basket. The Hot Air Balloon ascends, revealing aerial views of a valley with a river and urban areas, followed by green valleys, rocky peaks, and snow-dusted mountains. The narrator states that "here in New Mexico, the Rocky Mountains are far from dead" and suggests they "could still be growing." Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, identified on-screen, asserts that "portions of the Rocky Mountains are going up dynamically... and certainly in the million-year time frame we will have more interesting, higher and more rugged mountains," while the camera alternates between the passengers and the panoramic views of The Rockies.

Man in Gray Shirt and Cap.description: A man wearing a gray short-sleeved shirt with a logo on the chest and a beige cap, standing in the hot air balloon basket next to Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, holding a rope with a yellow glove, looking out at the scenery with a calm expression.

Balloon Basket.description: A metal-framed basket with visible chains and support structures, housing the passengers and burner system, shown from within and from an aerial perspective as the balloon ascends.

Balloon Envelope.description: The multi-colored fabric of the hot air balloon, featuring bold geometric patterns in yellow, red, blue, and green, shown inflating and expanding as it fills with hot air, with the interior illuminated by the burner's flame.

Hot Air Balloon.description: A large, colorful hot air balloon with a vibrant pattern of yellow, red, blue, and green panels, shown inflating with a powerful flame from the burner, ascending into the sky, and drifting over a valley with mountains in the distance, viewed from both inside and above. The narrator uses it as a metaphor for rising magma, and the distinct whooshing sound of its burner is heard during inflation.

Burner System.description: A metallic, industrial-looking burner emitting a powerful, bright orange flame to heat the balloon's envelope, shown in close-up and from within the balloon's interior, accompanied by a distinct whooshing sound as it ignites.

The Rockies.description: A vast mountain landscape featuring rugged peaks, deep valleys, and layered rock formations, captured through panoramic and aerial shots. Both the narrator and Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom discuss their geological activity, with the narrator stating they are 'far from dead' and 'still growing,' and Prof. Karlstrom predicting they will become 'taller, more spectacular,' and 'more rugged mountains' in the million-year time frame.

Arid Hillside Landscape.description: A dry, sun-baked hillside covered in sparse, golden-brown grass and low shrubs, with visible erosion patterns and exposed soil, serving as the fieldwork site for the geologists.

Aerial Landscape.description: An expansive view from above, showing a valley with roads, buildings, a river, green fields, and distant mountains, captured during the balloon's ascent, with sunlight reflecting off the river and casting shadows across the terrain.

Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom.description: An older man with light-colored hair, wearing a red jacket and sunglasses, standing in a hot air balloon basket, smiling and speaking. He is identified by an on-screen graphic and speaks in a clear, articulate voice, explaining his prediction that the Rocky Mountains are dynamically rising and will become 'more interesting, higher and more rugged' in the future.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a weathered, textured rock surface, showcasing natural erosion patterns in earthy tones, as a narrator explains that "helium 3" and "byproducts of activity in the mantle" indicate that "rock in the mantle is melting and on the move." The scene transitions to a wide shot of two individuals silhouetted atop a reddish-brown hill at sunset, with distant mesas, while the narrator continues, comparing the geological process to "a rising hot air balloon." This visual metaphor comes to life as the perspective shifts to the interior of a hot air balloon, where a powerful burner ignites with a distinct whooshing sound, filling the vibrant yellow, red, blue, and green envelope with hot air. From the basket, two passengers, including Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom in a red jacket, gaze out as the balloon ascends. Sweeping aerial views follow, revealing a sprawling valley with a river, urban areas, and distant mountains bathed in golden sunlight, then a lush green valley and rugged, rocky peaks, and finally snow-dusted mountain ridges and deep valleys. Throughout these grand vistas, accompanied by a calm background score, the narrator emphasizes that "here in New Mexico, the Rocky Mountains are far from dead," and "could still be growing, thrust higher by rising mantle." Prof. Karl E. Karlstrom, identified by an on-screen graphic, then speaks directly, stating in a clear, articulate voice, "I think portions of the Rocky Mountains are going up dynamically... and certainly in the million-year time frame we will have more interesting, higher and more rugged mountains, I would predict," as the camera alternates between close-ups of the passengers and the expansive panoramic views, capturing the serene beauty and scale of the terrain from above.

event_blocks.summary: The NAKED SCIENCE segment, featuring Prof. Brian Luckman of the University of Western Ontario, analyzes the "uncertain future" of the Rockies, a mountain range with a 200 million year history, highlighting the "bleak" outlook for North America's destiny due to human activity altering geological history. Prof. Luckman, identifiable by his white beard, wide-brimmed hat, and yellow backpack with a mounted device, uses GPS to map distances between annual moraine ridges, which mark the furthest points of glacial advance during the previous winter, to demonstrate how fast glaciers like the Columbia Icefield in Alberta, Canada, are melting. Time-lapse footage from 2001 to 2005 and dated photographs from 2006 and 2007, including those of the Athabasca Glacier and Grinnell Glacier in Montana, visually confirm the significant retreat of these glaciers, with Prof. Luckman explaining that these retreating moraines are a "tell-tale sign that the glacier is melting away." He emphasizes that "glaciers are one of the most effective ways to demonstrate to people that the climate is changing," noting that a mere two visits or comparison with older photographs reveal radical changes. The narrative underscores the scale and beauty of the landscape, from jagged peaks and expansive glaciers to lush valleys and meandering rivers, while somberly predicting the eventual disappearance of glaciers that have shaped the steep-sided peaks and U-shaped valleys, though water erosion will continue to sculpt the mountains.

Mist-Covered Mountain and Lake.description: A mountain range partially obscured by low-hanging clouds, with a calm lake in the foreground and dense evergreen forests along the shoreline, under an overcast sky, transitioning into a time-lapse with shifting light and moving clouds.

The Rockies.description: A vast mountain landscape featuring rugged peaks, deep valleys, and layered rock formations, captured through panoramic and aerial shots under a partly cloudy sky, emphasizing geological scale and natural beauty. A narrator explicitly states 'The Rockies are doomed' and discusses the 'Rocky Mountains' 200 million year history,' their 'dramatic birth,' 'complex evolution,' and 'uncertain future,' linking their fate to the North American continent.

Glacier.description: A massive, debris-covered glacier flowing through a U-shaped valley, with visible crevasses, meltwater streams, and a surface textured with rocks and ice, shown from aerial and ground-level perspectives. The audio identifies specific examples like the three-and-a-half-mile-long Athabasca Glacier and the Grinnell Glacier in Montana, highlighting their significant retreat and disappearance due to melting over decades.

Prof. Brian Luckman.description: An older man with a white beard, wearing a wide-brimmed hat adorned with patches, a blue jacket, and carrying a yellow backpack with a mounted device. He is seen walking across a rocky glacial terrain and later speaking directly to the camera with a concerned, experienced expression and a clear, authoritative voice with a distinct accent, explaining how glaciers are effective indicators of climate change.

NAKED SCIENCE.description: A recurring on-screen graphic in the lower third, featuring a blue and white design with a vertical line and the text 'NAKED SCIENCE' in bold white letters, consistently displayed during the field segment, identifying the program's branding.

Time-lapse Glacier Retreat Footage.description: A sequence of still images or video frames showing the same glacier landscape over the years 2001 to 2005, with the years displayed on screen. This footage is explicitly referenced by both Prof. Luckman and a narrator as crucial evidence of radical environmental change and the progressive retreat of glaciers like the Athabasca and Grinnell over decades.

raw_segments: The minute opens with a sweeping aerial view of a snow-covered mountain range, showcasing jagged peaks and expansive glaciers under a clear blue sky, as an ominous, serious musical score begins. A narrator states that geologists, having investigated the past, now look to a "bleak" future, declaring, "The Rockies are doomed, and North America's destiny hangs in the balance." The camera glides over deep crevasses and rocky outcrops, emphasizing the rugged landscape. As the scene transitions to a misty, golden-hued mountain vista at dawn or dusk, with layers of peaks emerging from low-lying clouds, the narrator continues, reflecting on the "Rocky Mountains' 200 million year history," their "dramatic birth," and "complex evolution," before turning to their "uncertain future" tied to the North American continent. The light shifts, and the camera pans across a lush valley with dense evergreen forests and a meandering river, leading to distant snow-capped mountains. The footage then focuses on a massive, debris-covered glacier nestled in a U-shaped valley. The narrator identifies this as "the Columbia Icefield in Alberta, Canada," where geologists are already observing how the mountains are changing, predicting that "within a hundred years, it could look something like this." The camera moves closer to reveal the glacier’s cracked, slowly flowing surface, with ground-level shots showing meltwater streams carving through the ice and pooling in reflective puddles, as the sun glints off the water. The narrator concludes with a somber note, stating that "human activity is altering the course of geological history," underscored by the dramatic and mournful music.

The segment opens with close-up shots of a rocky, debris-covered glacier surface, revealing crevasses and meltwater flowing through cracks, emphasizing the dynamic nature of the ice. A narrator's voice, accompanied by a serious background score, states that "increasing global temperatures are taking their toll on the mountains' glaciers," and "they're melting." The scene transitions to a wide panoramic view of the vast glacier in a mountain valley, with rugged peaks and clouds overhead, where a group of hikers can be seen walking across its surface, underscoring its immense scale. The narrator introduces "Brian Luckman from the University of Western Ontario," who "wants to know how fast" the glacier is melting, as a subtle helicopter or drone sound briefly passes. The camera then focuses on Prof. Brian Luckman, an older man with a white beard, wearing a wide-brimmed hat and a blue jacket, carrying a yellow backpack with a mounted device, walking across the rocky terrain. He is identified by an on-screen graphic as 'Prof. Brian Luckman, University of Western Ontario' under the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo. The narrator explains that "during the winter, glaciers grow," and an "advancing glacier pushes a pile of eroded rubble ahead of itself, called a moraine." The video includes time-lapse footage showing the glacier’s retreat from 2001 to 2005, with years appearing on screen to mark the progression, illustrating the environmental change. The narrator continues, explaining that "when the glacier shrinks back in summer, it leaves behind this pile of debris in its wake," which is "a tell-tale sign that the glacier is melting away." Prof. Luckman, speaking with a clear, slightly academic British/Canadian accent, elaborates on this, stating, "Because the glacier over time is retreating continuously, then what you have left are a series of these annual moraine ridges, which mark the furthest points of advance during the previous winter." The background music becomes slightly more prominent before the narrator concludes, describing Luckman walking "between the moraines, using GPS to map the distance between" them, all set against the consistent backdrop of a high-altitude, arid, rocky landscape with a large glacier under a partly cloudy sky, focusing on both the natural environment and the scientific observation of glacial retreat.

The video opens with a wide shot of a vast, debris-covered glacier in a mountainous valley, with a man in a blue jacket and wide-brimmed hat walking across the rocky foreground, as the year '2006' appears on screen. Ethereal, melancholic background music plays while a calm male narrator explains that "the greater the distance between them, the faster the glacier is melting." The scene transitions to a close-up of the same man, identified as Prof. Brian Luckman, an older man with a white beard, wearing a patched wide-brimmed hat and a yellow backpack. He speaks directly to the camera with a concerned, experienced expression and a distinct accent, stating, "Glaciers are one of the most effective ways to demonstrate to people that the climate is changing. You've only got to come here twice or look at some of the older photographs to realize something radically is changing." The footage then shifts to a series of aerial and panoramic views of glaciers and mountain ranges, showing the same glacier from different angles and time periods, including a 2007 timestamp and time-lapse footage from 2001-2005, highlighting its retreat. The narrator returns, detailing how "these two photographs of the three and a half mile long Athabasca Glacier show how far it has retreated over the past 30 years," and how "these photos of the Grinnell Glacier in Montana show that America's glaciers are disappearing as well." Dramatic shots of snow-covered peaks, deep valleys, and a turquoise glacial lake emphasize the landscape's scale and beauty, while the music swells. The sequence concludes with sweeping aerial views of forested valleys and snow-capped mountains under a clear blue sky, showcasing the broader ecosystem as the narrator somberly predicts that "one day, the glaciers that have shaped these steep-sided peaks and U-shaped valleys will disappear," though "water erosion will continue to sculpt these mountains."

event_blocks.summary: Sweeping aerial views of the sunlit Rockies reveal winding rivers, dense forests, snow-capped peaks, deep valleys, alpine lakes, and dramatic rock formations, as a narrator describes forces softening their jagged edges. Rockies geologist and author Ben Gadd, wearing a green cap and light beige jacket, stands before layered rock formations, explaining that the mountains erode at a rate of about 600ths of a millimeter per year, a measurement derived from river water sediment. The 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo appears before returning to aerial shots of snow-dusted peaks, glaciers, and turquoise lakes, accompanied by a building, dramatic orchestral score. A narrator states that the relentless force of erosion has sealed the fate of the Rocky Mountains since their rise, predicting they will eventually erode away to nothing. Prof. Stephen T. Johnston of the University of Victoria, wearing a red jacket and glasses, speaks in front of a forested backdrop, describing the erosion of the Rockies, the reason for their beauty and tourist appeal, noting that the draining rivers are full of silt and sand. The segment concludes with Prof. Johnston speaking again against a blurred, fast-moving natural background as the music continues.

Ben Gadd.description: An older man with a white beard, wearing a green cap with a mountain logo, a light beige jacket over a brown sweater, and khaki pants, speaking directly to the camera in a mountainous outdoor setting. He explains in a calm, clear, and explanatory voice that the Rockies are eroding at a rate of about 600ths of a millimeter per year, based on river water sediment analysis.

The Rockies.description: A vast mountain landscape featuring rugged peaks, deep valleys, and layered rock formations, captured through panoramic and aerial shots under a partly cloudy sky, emphasizing geological scale and natural beauty. Narrators and experts describe them as constantly eroding at a rate of about 600ths of a millimeter per year, with rivers full of silt and sand, highlighting their eventual fate of eroding away to nothing, despite being beautiful, dramatic, and a draw for tourists.

NAKED SCIENCE.description: A recurring on-screen graphic in the lower third, featuring a blue and white design with a vertical line and the text 'NAKED SCIENCE' in bold white letters, consistently displayed during the field segment, identifying the program's branding.

Prof. Stephen T. Johnston.description: A man with a bald head, glasses, and a graying beard, wearing a red jacket over a blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera in front of a forested backdrop, with the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo identifying him as 'Prof. Stephen T. Johnston, University of Victoria'. He speaks with a calm, explanatory tone, describing the Rockies as beautiful, dramatic, and a tourist attraction, while also emphasizing their ongoing erosion and the silt and sand carried by their rivers.

raw_segments: The minute opens with sweeping aerial shots of the vast, sunlit mountainous landscape of the Rockies, showcasing winding rivers, dense forests, rugged, snow-capped peaks, deep valleys, alpine lakes, and dramatic rock formations, all while a narrator describes forces "beating them down and softening their jagged edges." The scene transitions to Ben Gadd, a Rockies geologist and author, wearing a green cap and light beige jacket, standing before layered rock formations. He explains in a calm, clear voice that "the future of the Rockies is that they're eroding at the rate of about 600ths of a millimeter per year," a rate determined by measuring river water sediment. The 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo appears. The footage then cuts back to more sweeping aerial views of snow-dusted peaks, glaciers, and turquoise lakes, accompanied by a building, dramatic orchestral score. A narrator's voice returns, stating, "The relentless force of erosion has sealed the fate of the Rocky Mountains since they first began to rise," and later, "One day, they will erode away to nothing." Prof. Stephen T. Johnston from the University of Victoria, wearing a red jacket and glasses, appears speaking in front of a forested backdrop. The video briefly shows canoes gliding across a calm lake with mountains, followed by a dramatic waterfall cascading down a rocky cliff. Prof. Johnston, with a calm, explanatory tone, then describes how "The Rockies that we see today, the beautiful mountains, why tourists come here, why it's just uh one of the most dramatic places on the earth. Uh they're eroding away," noting that the draining rivers are "full of silt and sand." The segment concludes with Prof. Johnston speaking again, against a blurred, fast-moving natural background, as the music continues to play.

event_blocks.summary: Against a backdrop of the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo, Prof. Stephen T. Johnston, in a red jacket over a blue shirt, speaks of "bleeding to death" before the scene shifts to a satellite view of Earth, zooming into the Rockies. A narrator ominously states, "The Rockies will eventually dissolve away," as dramatic music plays, transitioning to a 3D topographical animation and then an aerial shot of a desert-like region where the narrator declares, "A continent ripped in two." The music swells as the narrator explains the Rio Grande Rift, a "great tear in the Earth's crust that could one day split North America in two," will be the legacy of the Rockies' erosion. The scene then focuses on a weathered metal sensor on a tripod in the desert, followed by Prof. Laura J. Crossey, in a floral shirt, speaking to the camera and crouching beside a wooden box with a solar panel, adjusting equipment. Nearby, a man in a straw hat and light shirt works on a laptop at a sunlit field research site with red soil. The narrative continues with a focus on a "five-year project" to monitor the widening of the Rio Grande Rift, with researchers setting up GPS stations in remote New Mexico. The narrator explains GPS receivers are buried five feet deep to move with the earth. Digital GPS coordinate overlays appear above researchers in straw hats, indicating a geolocation experiment. Prof. Mousumi Roy, identified by an on-screen graphic and wearing a white shirt, explains that two GPS stations provide information about relative motion, and a network allows for a detailed map of the rift's motions. The narrator notes the team expects the rift to widen between 0.5 and 2 millimeters annually, stating, "little by little, the continent is being unzipped."
