raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of Prof. Stephen T. Johnston, wearing a red jacket over a blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera against a forested backdrop, with the 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo visible, his voice heard saying "bleeding to death." The scene transitions to a sweeping satellite view of Earth, zooming into North America and then focusing on mountainous terrain, including the Great Lakes and the Rockies, as a male narrator's voice states, "The Rockies will eventually dissolve away," accompanied by dramatic, slightly ominous background music. The satellite view continues, shifting to a detailed 3D topographical animation of a rugged, green and brown landscape, while the narrator adds, "But far in the distant future, there could be a final devastating twist to their story." A real-world aerial shot then shows a desert-like region with sparse vegetation and rocky hills under a partly cloudy sky, panning across the dry, arid landscape with scattered shrubs and distant snow-capped peaks, as the narrator ominously declares, "A continent ripped in two." The melancholic background music becomes more prominent as the narrator continues, "The future of the Rocky Mountains is inevitable. Thanks to the relentless force of erosion, they will one day disappear. But they will leave a legacy: The Rio Grande Rift. A great tear in the Earth's crust that could one day split North America in two." The scene cuts to a close-up of a weathered, dome-shaped metal sensor mounted on a tripod in the desert. Then, Prof. Laura J. Crossey, identified by an on-screen graphic, is seen holding a handheld device and speaking to the camera, before crouching beside a wooden box with a solar panel, adjusting equipment. A man in a straw hat and light shirt sits nearby with a laptop, working on the setup in the bright, sunlit field research site with red soil and scattered greenery. The narrator introduces "Geologist Mazumi Roy and her colleague Nicholas George, both from the University of New Mexico," stating they "are taking part in an ambitious..." before the audio cuts off.

The segment opens with a close-up of a yellow handheld GPS device and a red wooden box with a solar panel, indicating a field research setup in a dry, arid landscape. A man in a straw hat and light shirt is seen working with the equipment, while a woman in a white shirt and khaki pants, later identified as Prof. Mousumi Roy, sits nearby using a laptop connected to the setup. A narrator explains this is a "five-year project" whose aim is "to monitor the widening of the Rio Grande Rift," as a documentary-style background score begins. The camera pulls back to reveal a wide, expansive desert scene with red soil, scattered shrubs, and distant mountains under a bright blue sky with fluffy white clouds. The narrator continues, detailing how "the team is setting up GPS stations along the length of the rift, like this one in remote New Mexico, three hours from Albuquerque." The focus shifts to a weathered metal sensor mounted on a tripod, showing signs of exposure, likely part of a remote monitoring station. The sound of digging or drilling is heard as the narrator explains that "the GPS receivers are buried in the bedrock to a depth of around five feet," moving when "the earth moves." The scene then transitions to a wide shot of the landscape where the man in the straw hat stands alone, followed by the woman joining him. As they stand together, digital overlays appear above them, displaying GPS coordinates, suggesting a scientific measurement or geolocation experiment. The 'NAKED SCIENCE' logo appears in the lower third, and a graphic introduces 'Prof. Mousumi Roy, University of New Mexico.' Prof. Roy then steps forward, holding a small black device, and begins to speak, explaining that with "two GPS stations," they gain "information about relative motion along a line that connects the two stations." The narrator adds that "a network of GPS stations allows the team to build up a much more detailed map of the rift's motions," before Prof. Roy continues, stating that "by looking at the difference between the coordinates between the GPS stations through time, we get a good idea of the relative motion between..."

The video opens with a wide shot of a dry, arid landscape under a bright blue sky with scattered clouds, where a woman in a white shirt and khaki pants, identified as Prof. Mousumi Roy, walks toward the camera while holding a small black GPS device. In the background, three men in straw hats and light-colored field attire stand spaced apart, each marked with a digital GPS coordinate overlay displaying precise latitude and longitude, indicating a geolocation experiment. A female narrator states, "Each of these stations," followed by a male narrator explaining that "The team has set up 24 GPS stations along the Rio Grande Rift." The scene transitions to a close-up of two researchers, both wearing straw hats, working beside a red wooden box equipped with a solar panel. One man adjusts a yellow GPS device connected to the box, while Prof. Roy uses a laptop on the ground, suggesting data collection or monitoring. The male narrator continues, noting, "It's early days, but the team expects to find that the rift is widening between 0.5 and 2 millimeters every year." After a whoosh sound, he adds, "It's a very slow process, but little by little, the continent is being unzipped." The camera then pans across sweeping desert vistas with red rock formations and eroded hills, emphasizing the remote, rugged terrain. The video cuts to an older man in a dark blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera with the desert landscape behind him, his face partially shadowed. He is heard explaining in an academic tone that "The Rio Grande Rift is a rift system that's causing a continent to split," and if this "process continues for tens of millions of years, you could eventually rift the North American continent apart." This is followed by Prof. Laura J. Crossey, identified by an on-screen graphic, a woman with long dark hair wearing a floral shirt, also speaking animatedly to the camera with a similar desert backdrop. She is heard stating, "There's a lot of indications that there's some action happening. Maybe some more is in the future for the Rocky Mountains and another few million years and there'll be greater tales to tell of active tectonism further to the north where this Rio Grande Rift is trying to work its way and crack the continent." The lighting remains bright and natural throughout, with the sun casting sharp shadows, indicating midday, all set to a subtle, atmospheric background music.

event_blocks.summary: A female voiceover briefly mentions an unsuccessful attempt before the view transitions from a rotating Earth from space, highlighting North and South America, to a topographical mountainous landscape. Upbeat, cinematic orchestral music swells as aerial perspectives reveal snow-covered mountain peaks under a clear blue sky, with the sun creating sharp shadows and lens flares. A male narrator introduces "The story of the Rocky Mountains," stating they "began with the birth of the North American continent" and could "end with its death." The camera moves over rugged, snow-dusted ridges, revealing a valley with a meandering river, green forests, and golden meadows, bordered by granite cliffs. The narrator hypothesizes about a distant future where the "Rio Grande Rift may split a continent," potentially dividing "North America" into "East and West America," but notes this is "hundreds of millions of years into the uncertain future." Aerial shots of the Sierra Nevada range, featuring Half Dome with snow and clouds, accompany the narrator's assertion that "Today, the iconic Rockies are in their prime." The sequence concludes with a view of a massive, snow-capped mountain range, possibly the Himalayas, with glaciers and jagged peaks under a blue sky, as the narrator declares these "dramatic and resilient" mountains, which "divide a continent," will "unite us all in admiration" and "remain the backbone of North America for millions of years."

event_blocks.summary: The segment commences with a sequence of black screens presenting white text credits, enumerating production personnel such as Narrator Demetri Goritsas, Director Kate Dart, Editor Jon Walker, and Cinematographer Rénald Bellemare, alongside various production staff and companies including Pioneer Productions and Handel Productions. This visual progression is underscored by a persistent, ambient instrumental music track, defined by sustained, reflective string sounds and a gentle, rhythmic percussion that emerges around the five-second mark, establishing a subtle melodic motif. The credits persist, acknowledging contributions from entities such as the National Science Foundation, National Geographic Film, and the Royal Bank of Canada, with particular gratitude extended to researchers and professors from academic and geological organizations. While the music sustains its atmospheric quality, the screen subsequently displays logos for Pioneer Productions, Handel Productions, Discovery Channel—characterized by its signature blue bar and rotating Earth graphic—and the Canadian Film or Video Production Tax Credit in conjunction with the Québec Film and Television Tax Credit. The segment culminates with a copyright notice for MMVIII (2008) before transitioning to a deep blue, textured background, evocative of an underwater or cosmic vista, which remains static as the instrumental music continues, indicating a pause or transition.
